In 1900, Max Planck proposed that energy: In 1900, Max Planck proposed that energy is quantized — it can only be emitted or absorbed in discrete packets called "quanta." The energy of each quantum is proportional to the frequency of the radiation, with h as the fundamental constant of proportionality.
In 1924, Louis de Broglie proposed that: In 1924, Louis de Broglie proposed that every moving particle has an associated wavelength, inversely proportional to its momentum. This was revolutionary — it meant that electrons, neutrons, and even atoms should produce interference patterns just like light! The same d·sinθ = mλ equation applies to all of them, you just plug in the de Broglie wavelength.

In PET scans (explored in this lab), the: In PET scans (explored in this lab), the annihilation of an electron and positron produces two 511 keV gamma rays. Using E = hc/λ, we find λ = 1240 eV·nm / (511 × 10³ eV) = 0.00243 nm — this is a gamma-ray wavelength. Detectors are specifically designed to catch these photons and reconstruct the 3D location of the annihilation event, creating the PET scan image.
In a pressure cooker, heating increases: In a pressure cooker, heating increases molecular KE, which directly raises pressure — the safety valve is critical because at 120°C the pressure is 2 atm, enough to burst a sealed container.
In forensic toxicology, the concentratio: In forensic toxicology, the concentration of alcohol in a blood sample is determined using the Beer-Lambert law. A spectrophotometer measures the absorbance of the sample at 340 nm after enzymatic reaction with NADH. The absorbance reading directly gives the ethanol concentration via A = εbc — the exact same principle demonstrated in this lab for the IR, NMR, and MS data.

In right triangles OAE and OCF: AE² = OA: In right triangles OAE and OCF: AE² = OA² − OE² and CF² = OC² − OF². Since OA = OC (radii) and OE = OF, we get AE² = CF², so AE = CF.
In right triangles OAE and OCF: OE² = OA: In right triangles OAE and OCF: OE² = OA² − AE² and OF² = OC² − CF². Since OA = OC (radii) and AE = CF, we get OE² = OF², so OE = OF.

Kepler discovered the elliptical orbits: Kepler discovered the elliptical orbits of planets empirically in 1609. But WHY are orbits elliptical? How did Newton derive Kepler's three laws from his universal law of gravitation?
Kepler's Third Law falls out directly fr: Kepler's Third Law falls out directly from equating centripetal acceleration to gravitational acceleration. The key insight: T² is proportional to a³ with a constant of proportionality that depends ONLY on the central mass M — NOT on the orbiting body's mass. This is why all planets in our solar system follow the same T²/a³ = constant relationship, and why we can calculate the mass of any central body just by measuring a satellite's orbit!

Newton predicted in the 1680s that a can: Newton predicted in the 1680s that a cannonball fired at 7.9 km/s would orbit Earth — 270 years before Sputnik 1 proved him right in 1957. The ISS now orbits at 7.66 km/s, exactly as Newton calculated.
Newton realized that the same force that: Newton realized that the same force that makes an apple fall also holds the Moon in orbit. The gravitational force is always attractive, central (pointing along the line connecting the two bodies), and follows an inverse-square law with distance. The minus sign indicates attraction — the force points opposite to the radial direction.

Newton's inverse-square law of gravity e: Newton's inverse-square law of gravity elegantly explains ALL of Kepler's empirical laws. The elliptical orbits (Kepler I) emerge from solving the orbital differential equation. Equal areas in equal times (Kepler II) come from angular momentum conservation. The T² ∝ a³ relation (Kepler III) follows directly from equating centripetal and gravitational forces. This unification of celestial and terrestrial mechanics was humanity's first grand unification of physics — showing that the same laws govern the fall of an apple and the motion of the planets.

Since AC = BC, C is the midpoint of chor: Since AC = BC, C is the midpoint of chord AB. The perpendicular from the centre to a chord bisects it. This is the converse of Theorem 9.2. Together, these two theorems are the foundation of all chord calculations — used in designing arches, bridges, and circular structures!
Since E and F are midpoints (Theorem 9.3: Since E and F are midpoints (Theorem 9.3): AB = 2×AE and CD = 2×CF. Since AE = CF, we get AB = CD.
Since O lies on the perpendicular bisect: Since O lies on the perpendicular bisector of AB, it's equidistant from A and B. Since it also lies on the bisector of BC, it's equidistant from B and C. Therefore OA = OB = OC = R.
Since OC = OE (radii), triangle COE is i: Since OC = OE (radii), triangle COE is isosceles. The exterior angle ∠COF = ∠CEO + ∠OCE = 2∠CEO. Similarly, ∠DOF = 2∠DEO.
Since PQ ⟂ OA, the distance from centre: Since PQ ⟂ OA, the distance from centre O to any point on PQ is at least OA (perpendicular distance is shortest). The distance from O to any other point on PQ is > OA = radius.
Since S = (a+b+c)/2 (semi-perimeter), we: Since S = (a+b+c)/2 (semi-perimeter), we get Δ = rS → r = Δ/S. The inradius is the ratio of the area to the semi-perimeter! For a right triangle with legs 3 and 4 (hypotenuse 5): Δ = 6, S = 6, so r = 1. The incircle has radius 1. This formula is used to find the radius of a pizza that fits exactly inside a triangular box!
Since both equal the same altitude: c \\: Since both equal the same altitude: c \sin α = a \sin γ → a/\sin α = c/\sin γ.
Since tan α = m₂ and tan β = m₁: tan θ =: Since tan α = m₂ and tan β = m₁: tan θ = (m₂ − m₁)/(1 + m₁m₂). Take absolute value for the acute angle. If tan θ = 0, lines are parallel. If 1 + m₁m₂ = 0, lines are perpendicular. Road engineers use this to design safe intersection angles!
Since the arcs are congruent, you can ro: Since the arcs are congruent, you can rotate the circle so that one arc exactly coincides with the other. The endpoints of the arcs match up.

Therefore, equal chords are equidistant: Therefore, equal chords are equidistant from the centre. Imagine two guitar strings (chords) of the same length — they're the same distance from the centre of the circular sound hole. This is used in engineering to balance forces in circular structures!

Wave-particle duality is not a paradox —: Wave-particle duality is not a paradox — it is the fundamental nature of quantum objects. Light and matter both exhibit wave properties (interference, diffraction) and particle properties (localized detection, momentum). The d·sinθ = mλ equation that you interact with in this lab is universal: it governs X-ray crystallography, electron microscopy, neutron scattering, and even the interference of large molecules like C₆₀. The wave nature determines where things CAN go; the particle nature determines where they DO go when measured.

We demonstrate a quantum eraser experime: We demonstrate a quantum eraser experiment using SPDC-generated entangled photon pairs. By marking the which-path information of one photon, the interference pattern of its twin is destroyed. Erasing this pathway information recovers the interference.
We engineer a biocompatible power source: We engineer a biocompatible power source inspired by the electric eel (Electrophorus electricus), using hydrogel-based artificial electrocytes. By stacking thousands of cells in series, we achieve up to 600 V output — directly demonstrating the Coulomb potential from charge separation.

lab.c12biochemistry_amino_acids_possess_: Amino acids possess an isoelectric point (pI) where they carry no net electrical charge. In a buffer solution, if pH < pI, the amino acid is protonated (positive) and migrates to the cathode (-). If pH > pI, it is deprotonated (negative) and migrates to the anode (+). At pH = pI, it remains stationary.

lab.final_formula_desc: The net force equals the rate of change of momentum
lab.gpe_adjust_desc: Adjust mass and height to see how Eₚ = mgh scales.
lab.gpe_correct_feedback: Correct! 73.6 J. A 2 kg book at the same height has only 29.4 J — less mass = less stored energy!
lab.gpe_incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Eₚ = m × g × h = 5 × 9.81 × 1.5
lab.gpe_practice_q: A 5 kg backpack sits on a shelf 1.5 m above the floor.
lab.gpe_practice_q2: What is its gravitational potential energy? (g = 9.81)
lab.gpe_real_life: Hydroelectric dams store water at height — every kilogram of water at 100 m has 981 J of gravitational PE, converted to electricity as it falls through turbines.
lab.gpe_step1_detail: 🎢 You're strapped into a roller coaster car, clicking up the first massive hill. The chain lift mechanism groans as it pulls you higher. Every meter you rise stores energy. E_p = Work = F × S — the motor does work against gravity to lift you.
lab.gpe_step2_detail: The force needed to lift the 500 kg car (plus you!) at constant speed equals its weight: F = mg. With two passengers, total mass ≈ 700 kg, so F = 700 × 9.81 = 6,867 N — about the weight of a small car.
lab.gpe_step3_detail: The first hill towers 45 meters above the ground — that's the vertical height S = h. The motor's work: W = 6,867 × 45 = 309,015 J. Every joule is stored as gravitational potential energy, waiting to be released.
lab.gpe_step4_detail: E_p = mgh = 700 × 9.81 × 45 = 309,015 J — enough energy to light a 60W bulb for 85 minutes! At the crest, you pause for a split second... then you plunge. That stored PE converts into kinetic energy as you race down, powering you through loops and corkscrews. The higher the first hill, the more energy for the entire ride!
lab.gpe_subtitle: How Eₚ = mgh comes from the definition of work
lab.hydraulic_adjust_desc: Adjust piston areas and input force to see force multiplication.
lab.hydraulic_correct_feedback: Correct! F₁ = 240 N. With a 50:1 mechanical advantage, 240 N lifts 12,000 N — that's the power of hydraulics!
lab.hydraulic_final_desc: Output force = area ratio × input force
lab.hydraulic_practice_q: Hydraulic lift: A₁ = 0.005 m², A₂ = 0.25 m². Car weight = 12,000 N.
lab.hydraulic_practice_q2: What minimum input force is needed?
lab.hydraulic_real_life: Car brakes use hydraulic multiplication — a small force on the brake pedal (5 cm² piston) creates 20× more force on the brake pads (100 cm² piston), letting your foot stop a 2-ton car.
lab.hydraulic_step1_detail: 🔧 You're a mechanic with a 1500 kg car that needs lifting. You place a hydraulic jack under the frame and pump the handle. Blaise Pascal discovered that pressure applied to a fluid in a closed container is transmitted equally in all directions. The pressure you create at the handle reaches the car undiminished.
lab.hydraulic_step2_detail: Pushing the small piston (area = 0.005 m²) with just 240 N of force creates P₁ = 240/0.005 = 48,000 Pa — like pressing a pen tip into your palm. A small area means big pressure from a small force.
lab.hydraulic_step3_detail: By Pascal's principle, the 48,000 Pa you created at the pump handle is transmitted through the hydraulic fluid WITHOUT LOSING ANY STRENGTH. P₂ = P₁ = 48,000 Pa. The fluid carries your force invisibly to where it's needed.
lab.hydraulic_step4_detail: F₂/A₂ = F₁/A₁, so F₂ = (A₂/A₁) × F₁ = (0.25/0.005) × 240 = 12,000 N! Your puny 240 N push lifts a full car. Mechanical advantage = 50×. This same principle stops your car when you press the brake pedal, controls massive excavators, and even powers dentist chairs!
lab.hydraulic_subtitle: How Pascal's principle lets a small force lift a heavy load
lab.incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Remember velocity changes direction!
lab.j.interact_laboratory_module: Interact with the laboratory module on the right to observe the outcomes.
lab.ke_adjust_desc: Adjust mass and velocity to see the v² effect first-hand.
lab.ke_correct_feedback: Correct! 200,000 J. At 40 m/s (144 km/h), it'd be 800 kJ — four times more! That's why speed is so dangerous.
lab.ke_final_desc: Kinetic energy = half × mass × velocity squared
lab.ke_incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Square velocity first: 20² = 400. Then E = 0.5 × 1000 × 400.
lab.ke_practice_q: A 1000 kg car travels at 20 m/s (72 km/h).
lab.ke_real_life: A car at 100 km/h has 4× the kinetic energy of the same car at 50 km/h. This is why speed limits exist — stopping distance grows with the square of speed, and crash energy quadruples when speed doubles.
lab.ke_step1_detail: 🛹 You're at the top of a massive half-pipe, heart pounding. You drop in, and gravity takes over. The work gravity does on you transforms into your kinetic energy: E_k = Work = F × d. The steeper the drop, the more work gravity does.
lab.ke_step2_detail: Gravity pulls you with force F = mg. The distance d you travel relates to your speed. Starting from rest, your average speed down the ramp is v_avg = v/2. So d = v_avg × t = (v/2) × t. E_k = (ma)(d) = m × a × (v/2 × t).
lab.ke_step3_detail: Your acceleration down the ramp is a = v/t (how quickly you reach speed v in time t). Plug it in: E_k = m × (v/t) × (v/2 × t). The t appears in the numerator AND denominator. Something beautiful is about to happen...
lab.ke_step4_detail: The time cancels! E_k = m × v × v/2 = ½mv². Doubling your speed means QUADRUPLING your energy. This is why car crashes at 100 km/h are 4× as destructive as at 50 km/h, and why dropping from twice the height on a skateboard doesn't just hurt twice as much — it hurts four times as much!
lab.ke_subtitle: How Eₖ = ½mv² comes from work and Newton's second law
lab.lp_adjust_desc: Adjust depth and density to see how P = ρgh works with real values.
lab.lp_correct_feedback: Correct! 301,658 Pa. That's ~3× atmospheric pressure! Submarines are built to withstand these forces.
lab.lp_incorrect_feedback: Not quite. P = ρgh = 1025 × 9.81 × 30. Multiply all three!
lab.lp_practice_q: A submarine dives in seawater (ρ = 1025 kg/m³) to 30 m.
lab.lp_practice_q2: What's the pressure at that depth? (g = 9.81 m/s²)
lab.lp_real_life: Submarines are built with thick hulls because at 300 m depth, water pressure is 30 atmospheres — enough to crush ordinary steel. The deeper you go, the more pressure increases.
lab.lp_step1_detail: 🏊 You jump off a boat into the ocean and dive down. Your ears feel an uncomfortable pressure building. Pressure = force / area — the column of water above you has weight, and that weight pushes on every square millimeter of your body.
lab.lp_step2_detail: The force is the weight of the water: F = mg. But m = ρV (density × volume). Seawater density is 1,025 kg/m³ — it's heavier than fresh water, which is why floating in the ocean is easier! F = ρ × V × g.
lab.lp_step3_detail: The volume of water above you = area of your body × depth: V = A × h. So F = ρ × A × h × g. Notice the area A appears — a wider person has more water weight above them. But something magical happens next...
lab.lp_step4_detail: P = F/A = (ρ × A × h × g)/A = ρgh. The area CANCELS! Pressure at a given depth is the same for everyone regardless of body shape. At just 10 m deep, pressure doubles to 2 atmospheres. Free divers train for years to handle this — the pressure crushes air spaces like your lungs and sinuses.
lab.lp_step4_label: Cancel Area to Get P = ρgh
lab.lp_subtitle: How P = ρgh emerges from the definition of pressure and weight
lab.lp_trace2: V = A × h, so P = (ρ × A × h × g) / A
lab.m10chordbisectors_a_chord_is_drawn_a: . A chord is drawn at a perpendicular distance of
lab.m10chordbisectors_a_circle_has_a_rad: A circle has a radius of
lab.m10chordbisectors_consequently_if_yo: Consequently, if you draw a line from the center of a circle to the midpoint of a chord, that line will always be perpendicular to the chord. This creates a right-angled triangle, allowing you to use the Pythagorean theorem to calculate missing lengths.
lab.m10chordbisectors_drag_points_a_b_an: Drag points A, B, and C along the circumference.

lab.m10chordbisectors_notice_how_the_per: Notice how the perpendicular bisectors of all three chords intersect precisely at the center of the circle!
lab.m10chordbisectors_of_a_chord_is_a_li: of a chord is a line that divides the chord into two equal parts at exactly a 90° angle.
lab.m10chordbisectors_the_perpendicular_: The perpendicular bisector of any chord of a circle always passes through the center of the circle.
lab.m10chordbisectors_what_is_the_total_: What is the total length of the chord?

lab.orbital_adjust_desc: Move the slider to see how orbit radius affects speed and period.
lab.orbital_correct_feedback: Correct! ~7,658 m/s. The ISS travels at 7.66 km/s — over 27,000 km/h, orbiting Earth 16 times per day!
lab.orbital_practice_q: ISS orbits at 408 km altitude. Earth radius = 6,371 km. T = 92.7 minutes.
lab.orbital_real_life: Geostationary satellites at 35,786 km orbit once every 24 hours, matching Earth's rotation so satellite TV dishes never need to move. Just 3 satellites can cover the entire globe.
lab.orbital_step1_detail: 🛰️ Imagine throwing a baseball horizontally from the top of a 100 km tall mountain. It arcs downward and lands far away. The further you want it to go, the faster you must throw. Average speed = distance / time — this simple idea is the seed of orbital mechanics.
lab.orbital_step2_detail: Now imagine throwing at 7.8 km/s — so fast that the Earth curves away beneath the ball at the same rate it falls! The ball never hits the ground — it orbits. In one full orbit, it travels the circumference of a circle: d = 2πr, where r is the distance from Earth's center.
lab.orbital_step3_detail: At 408 km above Earth (the ISS altitude), one complete orbit takes T = 92.7 minutes — that's the orbital period. The ISS crew sees 16 sunrises and sunsets every single day because they orbit so fast.
lab.orbital_step4_detail: v = 2πr/T = 2π × 6,779,000 m / 5,562 s ≈ 7,660 m/s — over 27,000 km/h! Higher orbits are slower: the Moon at 384,000 km orbits at just 1 km/s. The higher you go, the more leisurely your journey around Earth.
lab.orbital_subtitle: Derive v = 2πr/T from basic definitions of speed and circular motion
lab.orbital_title: Derivation: Orbital Speed of a Satellite
lab.p10_coulomb_formula_desc: Where k ≈ 8.99 × 10⁹ N·m²/C² (Coulomb's constant). Measurements include realistic sensor noise (±2%).
lab.p10_coulomb_incorrect_fb: Incorrect. Try rearranging the Coulomb's Law formula for q₂.
lab.p10_coulomb_q: In a separate experiment, charge q₁ is fixed at +4 μC. A force sensor measures 90.0 N of repulsive force when charge q₂ is placed exactly 2.0 cm away. What is the magnitude of q₂ in μC?
lab.p10_coulomb_subtitle: Investigate the electric force between two point charges.
lab.p10_coulomb_theory: Coulomb's Law states that the electrical force between two charged objects is directly proportional to the product of the quantity of charge on the objects and inversely proportional to the square of the separation distance between the two objects.
lab.p10_critical_adjust_desc: Adjust refractive indices to see critical angle change.
lab.p10_critical_correct_fb: Correct! 41.8°. Beyond this angle, light totally reflects inside glass — the principle behind optical fibers!
lab.p10_critical_final_desc: Critical angle determines the threshold for total internal reflection
lab.p10_critical_practice_q: Glass has n = 1.5 and air is n = 1.0. Find the critical angle for the glass-air boundary.
lab.p10_critical_real_life: Diamonds sparkle because they have a high refractive index (n=2.42), giving a critical angle of only 24°. Light entering gets trapped by total internal reflection, bouncing inside before escaping — creating brilliant fire!
lab.p10_critical_step1_detail: Swimming underwater, you look up at the surface. The world above appears as a shimmering circle. Light bends as it leaves water (n=1.33) into air (n=1.0). Snell's law n₁ sinθᵢ = n₂ sinθr describes exactly how much it bends.
lab.p10_critical_step2_detail: As you swim deeper and look up at a steeper angle, the bright circle shrinks. At a certain angle, the refracted ray runs along the surface at 90 degrees. This is the critical angle — beyond it, you can no longer see above the water.
lab.p10_critical_step3_detail: With sin 90 = 1, Snell's law simplifies: n₁ sinθc = n₂. The water surface turns into a perfect mirror. Below the critical angle, total internal reflection takes over.
lab.p10_critical_subtitle: Deriving θc from Snell's Law for Total Internal Reflection
lab.p10_echo_correct_fb: Correct! 300 m. The total distance traveled is 600 m (there and back), so the ocean floor is 300 m deep.
lab.p10_echo_final_desc: Depth = (Speed × Time) / 2 — used in sonar & ultrasound
lab.p10_echo_incorrect_fb: Not quite. Remember to divide by 2! d = v×t/2 = 1500×0.4/2
lab.p10_echo_practice_q: A ship sends a sonar pulse. The echo returns after 0.4 s. Sound in water = 1500 m/s.
lab.p10_echo_practice_q2: Find the depth of the ocean floor.
lab.p10_echo_real_life: Bats use echolocation — they emit ultrasonic chirps and listen for echoes. A 17 kHz squeak bouncing off a moth 1 m away returns in 6 ms, letting the bat pinpoint prey in total darkness.
lab.p10_echo_step1_detail: You're a sonar operator on a research ship mapping the ocean floor. You send a powerful ping into the deep. Speed = distance over time — the sound pulse travels through water at 1500 m/s, searching for the bottom.
lab.p10_echo_step2_detail: The ping hits the ocean floor and reflects back up. The total journey is there AND back: D = 2d. Your sound travels twice the target distance. You wait, listening for the echo...
lab.p10_echo_step3_detail: Your equipment measures exactly 0.4 seconds between sending the ping and receiving the echo. v = 2d/t means 1500 = 2d/0.4. The sound traveled 600 m total.
lab.p10_echo_step4_detail: d = v x t / 2 = 1500 x 0.4 / 2 = 300 m. Divide by 2 because the sound went down AND back up! This same formula maps the ocean floor, detects submarines, and creates ultrasound images.
lab.p10_echo_subtitle: Deriving the ultrasound depth formula from basic speed
lab.p10_elecenergy_correct_fb: Correct! 0.5 kWh. That's about 1.8 MJ of energy.
lab.p10_elecenergy_final_desc: Energy = Power × Time — how much electricity you consume
lab.p10_elecenergy_incorrect_fb: Not quite. E = P × t = 100 × 5 = 500 Wh. Convert to kWh by dividing by 1000.
lab.p10_elecenergy_practice_q: A 100W LED bulb runs for 5 hours. How much energy does it consume in kWh?
lab.p10_elecenergy_real_life: A typical UK home uses about 10 kWh per day. That's 36 MJ of electrical energy! Solar panels on your roof generate about 1 kWh per hour of peak sunlight per square meter.
lab.p10_elecenergy_step1_detail: Power tells us how fast energy is used. A 2000W air conditioner consumes energy at 2000 J per second. The longer it runs, the more energy it consumes.
lab.p10_elecenergy_step2_detail: P = E/t, so E = P × t. If you run the AC for 1 hour (3600 s), E = 2000 × 3600 = 7,200,000 J. That's a lot of energy!
lab.p10_elecenergy_step3_detail: Since P = V × I, we can write E = V × I × t. A 2000W AC running on 240V draws I = P/V = 2000/240 = 8.33 A. E = VIt = 240 × 8.33 × 3600 = 7.2 MJ.
lab.p10_elecenergy_step4_detail: E = VIt. This combines voltage, current, and time into one formula for electrical energy. 1 kilowatt-hour (kWh) = 3.6 MJ. Your electricity bill is based on kWh consumed.
lab.p10_elecenergy_subtitle: Deriving energy from power and time
lab.p10_escope_angle: The angle of deflection θ is roughly proportional to the square of the charge on the leaf.
lab.p10_escope_conduction: The angle of deflection θ is roughly proportional to the square of the charge on the leaf.
lab.p10_escope_correct_fb: Correct! The positive rod attracts electrons up to the disc, leaving the leaf less negative, so the repulsion (and angle) decreases.
lab.p10_escope_hint: What happens to the electrons on the leaf when a positive rod is brought near the disc?
lab.p10_escope_incorrect_fb: Incorrect. Try simulating it! Give the electroscope negative charge, then bring a positive rod near.
lab.p10_escope_induction: If the rod touches the disc (Conduction), electrons are transferred, leaving the electroscope permanently charged.
lab.p10_escope_q: A neutral electroscope is touched by a negatively charged rod, then the rod is removed. A positively charged rod is then brought near the disc (not touching). Does the leaf angle increase, decrease, or remain the same?
lab.p10_escope_subtitle: Detect electric charge via induction and conduction.
lab.p10_escope_theory: An electroscope detects charge. When a charged rod is brought near the brass disc, it induces a charge separation (Induction). Electrons are repelled or attracted, leaving the gold leaf with a net charge that causes it to repel from the fixed brass stem.
lab.p10_expLiq_adjust_desc: Adjust expansion coefficients to see real vs apparent relationship.
lab.p10_expLiq_correct_fb: Correct! 1.45×10⁻⁴/°C. Real expansion is always larger than apparent.
lab.p10_expLiq_practice_q: A liquid's apparent expansion is γ_a = 1.2×10⁻⁴/°C. Glass expansion is γ_g = 2.5×10⁻⁵/°C. Find γ_r.
lab.p10_expLiq_real_life: Thermometers use mercury or alcohol expansion — a 1°C rise expands liquid up a narrow capillary. Mercury thermometers work from -39°C to 357°C.
lab.p10_expLiq_step1_detail: On a hot summer day, you check the outdoor thermometer. The red liquid climbs up the glass tube. But here is the trick: the glass itself also expands. You are only seeing the APPARENT expansion of the liquid.
lab.p10_expLiq_step2_detail: Gamma-r = Gamma-a + Gamma-g. The real expansion of the liquid (Gamma-r) equals what you observe (Gamma-a) plus the glass flask expansion (Gamma-g). The glass hides some of the liquid's true expansion.
lab.p10_expLiq_step3_detail: Since Gamma-r is greater than Gamma-a always, the real expansion is always bigger than what you see. The glass container grows too, making extra room for the liquid.
lab.p10_expLiq_title: Derivation: Real vs Apparent Expansion of Liquids
lab.p10_halflife_def: The half-life (T_½) is the time required for exactly half of the radioactive nuclei in a sample to decay.
lab.p10_halflife_incorrect_fb: ✗ Incorrect. Hint: How many half-lives to go from 800 to 100?
lab.p10_halflife_q: A radioactive sample starts with 800 atoms. After exactly 12 seconds, a detector counts 100 active atoms remaining. What is the half-life of this isotope in seconds?
lab.p10_halflife_subtitle: Quantitative analysis of radioactive decay curves.
lab.p10_halflife_theory: Radioactive decay is a random process. The rate of decay is proportional to the number of active nuclei N remaining:
lab.p10_linExp_adjust_desc: Adjust values to see how rods expand with temperature.
lab.p10_linExp_correct_fb: Correct! 7.2 mm. Over 1 km of rail, that's 36 cm — why expansion gaps exist!
lab.p10_linExp_incorrect_fb: Not quite. ΔL = α × L₀ × ΔT. ΔT = 45-15 = 30°C.
lab.p10_linExp_practice_q: A steel rail is 20 m long at 15°C. In summer it reaches 45°C. α_steel = 1.2×10⁻⁵/°C. How much does it expand?
lab.p10_linExp_real_life: Railway tracks have expansion gaps — steel expands by 0.012 mm/m/°C. A 1 km track on a 40°C day grows 12 cm longer than in winter.
lab.p10_linExp_step1_detail: On a scorching summer day, train tracks can buckle under the heat. The change in length is proportional to original length and temperature change.
lab.p10_linExp_step2_detail: Engineers use expansion joints to prevent buckling. α is the coefficient of linear expansion. Steel has α = 1.2e-5 per °C.
lab.p10_linExp_step3_detail: α = ΔL/(L₀·ΔT). A 100 m rail heating from 15°C to 45°C expands by 3.6 cm.
lab.p10_linExp_step4_detail: L = L₀(1 + α·ΔT). Invar barely expands — used in precision instruments. Bimetallic strips in thermostats use two metals to bend with heat.
lab.p10_linExp_subtitle: Deriving α the coefficient of linear thermal expansion
lab.p10_logic_desc: Select the MYSTERY GATE and test different inputs to construct its truth table.
lab.p10_logic_subtitle: Test different logic gates and observe their truth tables with continuous voltages.
lab.p10_logic_theory: In real electronic circuits, logic gates process continuous voltages. TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic) defines specific voltage thresholds.
lab.p10_logic_theory2: HIGH (1): Between 2.0V and 5.0V | LOW (0): Between 0.0V and 0.8V | Undefined: Between 0.8V and 2.0V
lab.p10_lorentz_desc: Calculate the unknown mass m. Slope of I vs tan(θ) = (B·L)/(m·g). Use g = 9.81 m/s².
lab.p10_lorentz_subtitle: Measure the magnetic force on a current-carrying wire in a uniform magnetic field.
lab.p10_lorentz_theory1: A wire of length L carrying current I in a magnetic field B experiences a magnetic force Fm:
lab.p10_lorentz_theory2: When the wire hangs like a pendulum of mass m, gravity pulls it down. At equilibrium, the angle θ is given by:
lab.p10_lorentz_title: Unit 17: Lorentz Force & DC Motor Principle
lab.p10_mag_adjust_desc: Adjust values to see magnification from both methods.
lab.p10_mag_correct_fb: Correct! I = 4 cm (inverted). M = -2 means twice as tall and inverted!
lab.p10_mag_final_desc: Magnification = Image height / Object height = Image distance / Object distance
lab.p10_mag_incorrect_fb: M = q/p = 30/15 = 2. Image height = M×O = 2×2 = 4 cm
lab.p10_mag_practice_q: An object 2 cm tall is placed 15 cm from a concave mirror. Image forms 30 cm in front. Find magnification and image height.
lab.p10_mag_real_life: Microscopes use two lenses — objective creates magnified real image, eyepiece magnifies further. Compound microscopes achieve 1000× magnification.
lab.p10_mag_step1_detail: You're examining a tiny antique watch with a magnifying glass. Magnification M = I/O. The lens makes a small thing look big.
lab.p10_mag_step2_detail: From ray diagram geometry, similar triangles reveal M = q/p. The lens creates a virtual image that your brain interprets as larger.
lab.p10_mag_step3_detail: M = I/O = q/p. For a concave mirror, q is negative for real images.
lab.p10_mag_subtitle: Deriving M = I/O = q/p from geometry
lab.p10_magfield_desc: Calculate the slope of your B vs 1/x³ graph to find the unknown magnetic moment. Slope = 0.2 × m.
lab.p10_magfield_formula_desc: Where m is the magnetic moment and μ₀/4π = 10⁻⁷ T·m/A. Our magnetometer measures the field in microteslas (μT).
lab.p10_magfield_subtitle: Investigate the inverse-cube law of a magnetic dipole on its axial line.
lab.p10_magfield_theory: The magnetic field B on the axial line of a short bar magnet (a magnetic dipole) at distance x from its center is:
lab.p10_magfield_title: Unit 17: Magnetic Field of a Dipole
lab.p10_ohm_correct_fb: Correct! The true resistance is {r} Ω.
lab.p10_ohm_formula2: By plotting a graph of Voltage (V) vs Current (I), the gradient (slope) of the line of best fit represents the Resistance (R).
lab.p10_ohm_subtitle: Determine unknown resistance by plotting a V-I graph.
lab.p10_ohm_theory: Ohm's Law states that current (I) is directly proportional to voltage (V) in an ohmic conductor.
lab.p10_par_adjust_desc: Adjust resistors to see parallel equivalent.
lab.p10_par_correct_fb: Correct! 50Ω. Two equal resistors in parallel give half the value.
lab.p10_par_practice_q: Two resistors 100Ω and 100Ω are in parallel. Find equivalent resistance.
lab.p10_par_real_life: House wiring is parallel so each outlet gets full 230V independently. Unplugging one doesn't cut power to others.
lab.p10_par_step1_detail: You flip a switch and the ceiling light turns on. Add another lamp — both stay bright because they are wired in parallel. Total current splits among branches: I = I₁ + I₂ + I₃.
lab.p10_par_step2_detail: Using Ohm's law for each branch: V/Req = V/R₁ + V/R₂ + V/R₃. Voltage is the same across all parallel components.
lab.p10_par_step3_detail: Divide by V: 1/Req = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃. For two 100Ω resistors: 1/Req = 2/100, so Req = 50Ω. Adding paths DECREASES resistance.
lab.p10_photon_adjust_desc: Adjust frequency to see photon energy and momentum.
lab.p10_photon_correct_fb: Correct! 3.61×10⁻¹⁹ J (2.25 eV). Photon energy is incredibly tiny at human scale!
lab.p10_photon_final_desc: Photon energy and momentum from Planck and de Broglie
lab.p10_photon_practice_q: Green light has f = 5.45×10¹⁴ Hz. h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴. Find photon energy.
lab.p10_photon_real_life: Remote controls use infrared LEDs at 940 nm — each photon carries 2.1×10⁻¹⁹ J. Your TV detects thousands per second.
lab.p10_photon_step1_detail: Max Planck discovered light comes in tiny packets called photons. E = hf — each photon's energy equals Planck constant times frequency.
lab.p10_photon_step2_detail: For light, c = fλ so f = c/λ. Red light (700nm) has low frequency. UV light (below 380nm) has higher frequency — more energetic photons.
lab.p10_photon_subtitle: Deriving E = hf = hc/λ and p = h/λ
lab.p10_potDiv_adjust_desc: Adjust resistors to see how voltage divides.
lab.p10_potDiv_final_desc: Output voltage depends on the ratio of resistances
lab.p10_potDiv_practice_q: A 9V battery with R₁ = 1kΩ, R₂ = 500Ω. What is V_out across R₂?
lab.p10_potDiv_real_life: Volume knobs in audio equipment are potential dividers — sliding contact moves along a resistive track, controlling loudness.
lab.p10_potDiv_step1_detail: You turn the volume knob on your speakers. Inside is a potentiometer — a variable resistor that divides voltage. I = V_in/(R₁+R₂).
lab.p10_potDiv_step2_detail: The voltage you want is across R₂. Using Ohm's law: V_out = I × R₂. Same current flows through both resistors.
lab.p10_potDiv_step3_detail: V_out = R₂/(R₁+R₂) × V_in. The voltage divides proportionally to the resistance ratio. Turn the knob — volume changes!
lab.p10_power_correct_fb: Correct! I = 6.25 A. That's why kettles need thick wires — they draw high current!
lab.p10_power_final_desc: Power = Voltage × Current — the rate of energy transfer in a circuit
lab.p10_power_practice_q: A 1.5 kW kettle runs on 240 V. How much current does it draw?
lab.p10_power_real_life: Power companies charge for kilowatt-hours (kWh). 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ. A 100W bulb left on for 10 hours uses 1 kWh. Your monthly bill = total kWh × rate.
lab.p10_power_step1_detail: Power tells you how fast electrical energy is being used. A 60W bulb converts 60 J of electrical energy into light and heat every second. Power = Energy / Time — the rate of energy transfer.
lab.p10_power_step2_detail: Voltage V is the energy per unit charge: V = E/Q. One volt means 1 J of energy per coulomb of charge. A 12V battery gives 12 J of energy to each coulomb that flows.
lab.p10_power_step3_detail: Current I is the charge flowing per second: I = Q/t. One ampere = 1 coulomb per second. The total charge that flows carries the energy that gets converted.
lab.p10_power_step4_detail: P = E/t = (V × Q)/t = V × (Q/t) = V × I. Power = Voltage × Current. A 60W bulb at 120V draws I = P/V = 0.5 A. Your phone charger at 5V, 2A delivers 10W of power.
lab.p10_power_subtitle: Deriving power formula from voltage and current definitions
lab.p10_radDecay_adjust_desc: Adjust half-life and time to see exponential decay.
lab.p10_radDecay_final_desc: Exponential decay law for radioactive nuclei
lab.p10_radDecay_practice_q: A sample starts with 800 nuclei, half-life = 5 years. How many remain after 15 years?
lab.p10_radDecay_real_life: Smoke detectors contain americium-241 (432-year half-life). It ionizes air between plates; smoke disrupts this, triggering the alarm.
lab.p10_radDecay_step1_detail: An archaeologist discovers an ancient wooden tool. Carbon-14 dating holds the answer. After one half-life (5,730 years), exactly half remain: N₁ = ½ N₀.
lab.p10_radDecay_step2_detail: After two half-lives (11,460 years), only a quarter remains: N₂ = (½)² N₀.
lab.p10_radDecay_step3_detail: After n half-lives: N = (½)ⁿ N₀ = N₀/2ⁿ. This is exponential decay.
lab.p10_radDecay_step4_detail: N = N₀/2^(t/T½). If the tool has 25% of original C-14, n = 2 half-lives = 11,460 years old.
lab.p10_radDecay_subtitle: Deriving exponential decay law N = N₀/2ⁿ
lab.p10_resist_adjust_desc: Adjust wire properties to see resistivity in action.
lab.p10_resist_correct_fb: Correct! 1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m — copper's resistivity, standard for electrical wiring!
lab.p10_resist_practice_q: A copper wire 5 m long, A = 1×10⁻⁶ m², has R = 0.084 Ω. Find resistivity.
lab.p10_resist_real_life: Heating elements use nichrome (ρ=1.1×10⁻⁶ Ω·m) — high resistivity converts electricity to heat. Copper (ρ=1.7×10⁻⁸) carries current without heating.
lab.p10_resist_step1_detail: An electrician wires a new house. R ∝ L/A. Longer wires resist more; thicker wires resist less.
lab.p10_resist_step2_detail: R = ρ × L/A. ρ is resistivity — the material's inherent opposition to current. Copper ρ = 1.68×10⁻⁸ Ω·m.
lab.p10_resist_step3_detail: ρ = R × A / L. Silver has lowest resistivity (1.59×10⁻⁸) but is too expensive. Copper is the practical champion.
lab.p10_resist_subtitle: Deriving resistivity from resistance and geometry
lab.p10_ser_adjust_desc: Adjust resistors to see series equivalent.
lab.p10_ser_correct_fb: Correct! 1000Ω. Current I = 10/1000 = 10 mA through all three.
lab.p10_ser_final_desc: Sum of all resistances in series
lab.p10_ser_practice_q: Three resistors 200Ω, 300Ω, 500Ω in series with 10V battery. Find total resistance.
lab.p10_ser_real_life: Old Christmas lights wired in series go dark when one bulb fails — the broken bulb breaks the only current path.
lab.p10_ser_step1_detail: Remember old Christmas lights where if ONE bulb burned out, the WHOLE string went dark? That is series wiring. V = V₁ + V₂ + V₃.
lab.p10_ser_step2_detail: Using Ohm's law: I×Req = I×R₁ + I×R₂ + I×R₃. Same current flows through every bulb — only one path.
lab.p10_ser_step3_detail: Req = R₁ + R₂ + R₃. Three 100Ω bulbs in series give 300Ω total. More bulbs = higher resistance = dimmer each becomes.
lab.p10_ser_subtitle: Deriving R_eq = R₁ + R₂ + R₃ for series
lab.p10_shElec_adjust_desc: Adjust values to see electrical calorimetry determine specific heat.
lab.p10_shElec_correct_fb: Correct! ~780 J/kg·K — typical for vegetable oils!
lab.p10_shElec_final_desc: Specific heat of liquid from electrical heating
lab.p10_shElec_incorrect_fb: Use c_l = (VIt - m_c c_c ΔT) / (m_l ΔT)
lab.p10_shElec_practice_q: A 12V, 3A heater runs 150s in a calorimeter (m_c=0.08kg, c_c=900) containing 0.3kg liquid. Temp rises 22°C to 42°C. Find specific heat.
lab.p10_shElec_real_life: An electric kettle heats 1L water from 20°C to 100°C needing 336,000 J. At 2000W, that takes about 168 seconds.
lab.p10_shElec_step1_detail: You fill an electric kettle and switch it on. The heating element converts electrical energy to heat: Q_heater = V×I×t.
lab.p10_shElec_step2_detail: Heat warms both the water AND the metal calorimeter: Q_heater = m_l c_l ΔT + m_c c_c ΔT.
lab.p10_shElec_step3_detail: c_l = (VIt - m_c c_c ΔT) / (m_l ΔT). Subtract calorimeter share, divide by water mass and temperature rise.
lab.p10_shElec_subtitle: Deriving c_l using electrical heating and calorimetry
lab.p10_shMix_adjust_desc: Adjust values to see how calorimetry determines specific heat.
lab.p10_shMix_final_desc: Specific heat of solid from mixture method
lab.p10_shMix_incorrect_fb: Use c_s = (m_w c_w + m_c c_c)(T₃ - T₁) / (m_s(T₂ - T₃))
lab.p10_shMix_practice_q: A 0.2kg metal at 90°C in 0.15kg water at 25°C. Calorimeter: 0.08kg, c_c=900. Final temp=28°C. Find c_s.
lab.p10_shMix_real_life: Calorimetry in chemistry labs uses this principle — mixing known masses at known temperatures to find specific heat of unknown materials.
lab.p10_shMix_step1_detail: You're making tea. Pour hot water into a cup and add cold milk. Heat lost = heat gained.
lab.p10_shMix_step2_detail: Q_s = m_s c_s (T₂ - T₃). The hot solid cools, losing heat.
lab.p10_shMix_step3_detail: Q_w + Q_c = (m_w c_w + m_c c_c)(T₃ - T₁). Both liquid and container absorb heat.
lab.p10_shMix_step4_detail: c_s = (m_w c_w + m_c c_c)(T₃ - T₁) / (m_s (T₂ - T₃)). All values measurable in lab.
lab.p10_shMix_subtitle: Deriving c_s using calorimetry and conservation of energy
lab.p10_tempCo_adjust_desc: Adjust values to find temperature coefficient.
lab.p10_tempCo_practice_q: Copper ρ₀=1.68×10⁻⁸ at 20°C. At 70°C, ρ_T=2.01×10⁻⁸. Find α.
lab.p10_tempCo_real_life: Incandescent bulbs have cold resistance ~40Ω but hot resistance ~400Ω — 10× increase explains the surge current when switched on.
lab.p10_tempCo_step1_detail: Your phone dies faster on a freezing day. Resistance changes with temperature. Change in resistivity ∝ initial resistivity × ΔT.
lab.p10_tempCo_step2_detail: ρ_T - ρ₀ = α ρ₀ ΔT. For copper, α is positive — resistance INCREASES when hot.
lab.p10_tempCo_step3_detail: α = (ρ_T - ρ₀)/(ρ₀ ΔT). Copper α = 0.0039/°C. A copper wire heating 50°C increases resistance by 19.5%.
lab.p10_tempCo_step4_detail: ρ_T = ρ₀[1 + α(T - T₀)]. Carbon and semiconductors have NEGATIVE α — used in thermistors for digital thermometers.
lab.p10_tempCo_subtitle: Deriving the temperature coefficient α of resistivity
lab.p10_transGain_adjust_desc: Adjust currents to see transistor gain.
lab.p10_transGain_correct_fb: Correct! β = 200. 0.05 mA controls 10 mA — 200× amplification!
lab.p10_transGain_practice_q: A transistor has I_B = 0.05 mA and I_C = 10 mA. Find β.
lab.p10_transGain_real_life: Hearing aids use transistors with β of 100+ — a tiny microphone signal (μA) drives a speaker at mA levels, amplifying 100×.
lab.p10_transGain_step1_detail: At a concert, the singer whispers into a mic and it fills the stadium. Inside, a tiny transistor amplifies. I_E = I_B + I_C.
lab.p10_transGain_step2_detail: β = I_C/I_B. Current gain typically 50-400. Tiny base current controls much larger collector current.
lab.p10_transGain_step3_detail: α = I_C/I_E, always slightly less than 1 (0.95-0.99). α = β/(β+1).
lab.p10_transGain_step4_detail: I_C = β × I_B. A 1μA base change produces 200μA collector change if β=200. Your smartphone has billions amplifying!
lab.p10_transGain_subtitle: Deriving β = I_C/I_B and α = I_C/I_E
lab.p10_trans_adjust_desc: Adjust voltages/current to see power conservation.
lab.p10_trans_correct_fb: Correct! 20A. Voltage steps down 10×, current steps up 10×. 480W = 480W.
lab.p10_trans_practice_q: Step-down: V_p=240V, I_p=2A, V_s=24V. Find I_s.
lab.p10_trans_real_life: Phone chargers use step-down transformers — 230V at wall becomes 5V at USB. Turns ratio 46:1 reduces voltage safely.
lab.p10_trans_step1_detail: Power plants generate at 25,000V. Transformers step up to 500,000V. Ideal transformer conserves power: Power_in = Power_out.
lab.p10_trans_step2_detail: P = IV for both coils: I_p V_p = I_s V_s. High voltage = low current = low I²R heating losses.
lab.p10_trans_step3_detail: I_s = (V_p/V_s) × I_p. Step-down transformer (500kV→230V) has V_p/V_s ≈ 2174. Current multiplies by 2174.
lab.p10_trans_step4_detail: V_s/V_p = N_s/N_p = I_p/I_s. The voltage ratio equals turns ratio, inverse of current ratio.
lab.p10_trans_subtitle: Deriving I_p V_p = I_s V_s from conservation of power
lab.p10_volExp_adjust_desc: Adjust values to see volume expansion.
lab.p10_volExp_correct_fb: Correct! 0.011 m³ (11 L). Liquids expand more than solids!
lab.p10_volExp_practice_q: A 0.5 m³ alcohol tank at 10°C heats to 30°C. β=1.1×10⁻³/°C. Find ΔV.
lab.p10_volExp_real_life: Car engine thermostats use a wax pellet that expands with temperature — at 88°C it melts, opening the coolant valve to prevent overheating.
lab.p10_volExp_step1_detail: On a hot day, the fuel in your car's gas tank expands. ΔV ∝ V₀ × ΔT. Gas station tanks are calibrated for this.
lab.p10_volExp_step2_detail: ΔV = β V₀ ΔT. β is coefficient of volumetric expansion. Gasoline β ≈ 9.5×10⁻⁴/°C.
lab.p10_volExp_step3_detail: β = ΔV/(V₀ ΔT). For isotropic solids, β ≈ 3α. Volume = L³, so if length expands by (1+αΔT), volume expands by about (1+3αΔT).
lab.p10_volExp_step4_detail: β = 3α. This is why hot air balloons rise — air inside expands, becomes less dense, and floats.
lab.p10_volExp_subtitle: Deriving β the coefficient of volumetric expansion
lab.p10_waveeq_correct_fb: Correct! ~343 m/s. That's the speed of sound in air at 20°C!
lab.p10_waveeq_final_desc: Speed = Frequency × Wavelength — universal for all waves
lab.p10_waveeq_practice_q: A sound wave has f = 440 Hz (middle A) and λ = 0.78 m. Find the speed of sound.
lab.p10_waveeq_real_life: Tuning a guitar string to 82 Hz (low E) with length 0.65 m means the wave speed is 107 m/s. Tightening the string raises the wave speed, increasing frequency — that's how you tune a guitar.
lab.p10_waveeq_step1_detail: You're at the beach watching waves roll in. Wave speed = distance over time: v = d/t.
lab.p10_waveeq_step2_detail: In one complete wave cycle, a wave travels exactly one wavelength in one time period T. Substituting: v = λ/T.
lab.p10_waveeq_step3_detail: Frequency f = 1/T. Higher frequency means more waves per second. Light waves have enormous frequencies — 5e14 Hz for green light.
lab.p10_waveeq_step4_detail: v = f × λ. This is the UNIVERSAL wave equation — it works for ALL waves. Sound, light, radio, water waves all obey v = fλ!
lab.p10_waveeq_subtitle: Deriving the universal wave equation from basic speed formula
lab.p10_waveeq_title: Derivation: Universal Wave Equation v = fλ
lab.p10absorbersreflectors_both_cans_sta: Both cans start at room temperature (20°C). The heater emits infrared radiation equally in both directions.
lab.p10absorbersreflectors_different_sur: Different surfaces absorb thermal radiation at different rates. To test this, a radiant heater is placed exactly halfway between two metal cans: one painted
lab.p10absorbersreflectors_incorrect_hin: ✗ Incorrect. Hint: Base is 20°C, and it rises by 0.2°C per Watt.
lab.p10absorbersreflectors_set_the_heate: Set the heater power, then scrub the time slider to see how temperatures change over 30 minutes.
lab.p10absorbersreflectors_the_rate_of_t: The rate of temperature rise decreases as the can loses heat to the surroundings, eventually reaching thermal equilibrium.
lab.p10absorbersreflectors_using_a_heate: Using a heater power of 300W, what is the theoretical maximum temperature the Matt Black can will reach? (Assume it runs forever. Enter nearest whole number).
lab.p10carbonfootprint_are_found_by_divi: are found by dividing the total emissions by the number of passengers.
lab.p10carbonfootprint_calculate_the_per: Calculate the per-passenger CO₂ footprint (in kg) for a 15 km trip in an SUV (0.35 kg/km) carrying 3 passengers.
lab.p10carbonfootprint_combustion_of_fos: Combustion of fossil fuels in vehicles releases Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), a greenhouse gas. The total emissions depend on the distance traveled and the vehicle's emission factor.
lab.p10carbonfootprint_incorrect_check_y: Incorrect. Check your math: (Distance × Factor) / Passengers.
lab.p10convectioncurrents_given_v_kp_c_u: Given $v = kP + c$, use your plotted data to calculate the proportionality constant $k$ (the slope). Enter to 3 decimal places.
lab.p10convectioncurrents_incorrect_hint: ✗ Incorrect. Hint: Pick two points and calculate (v2-v1)/(P2-P1).
lab.p10convectioncurrents_is_the_transfe: is the transfer of thermal energy through a fluid (liquid or gas) by the upward movement of warmer, less dense regions of fluid.
lab.p10convectioncurrents_set_the_heater: Set the heater power and start the experiment. The purple dye traces the convection current. Measure the time it takes to reach the top.
lab.p10convectioncurrents_when_the_water: When the water at the bottom of the beaker is heated, it expands, its density decreases, and it rises. Cooler, denser water sinks to replace it, forming a continuous convection cell.
lab.p10derivationspecificheatelectrical_: A 12V, 3A heater runs for 150s in a calorimeter (m_c=0.08 kg, c_c=900 J/kg·K) containing 0.3 kg liquid. Temp rises from 22°C to 42°C.
Adjust values to see electrical calorimetry determine specific heat.

An electric kettle heats 1 liter of water (1 kg) from 20°C to 100°C — needing 336,000 J. At 2000 W, that takes about 168 seconds: the high specific heat of water makes it excellent for storing thermal energy.
— typical for vegetable oils! Different liquids have unique specific heats.

lab.p10electroscope_correct_the_positive: Correct! The positive rod attracts electrons up to the disc, leaving the leaf less negative, so the repulsion (and angle) decreases. (Or if it was neutral, it diverges/repels).

lab.p12thermomechanics_subrahmanyan_chan: Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar determined that a white dwarf cannot support itself if its mass exceeds a certain limit. Based on the simulation, at what exact mass (in M_sun) does the star completely collapse?
lab.p12thermomechanics_superconductors_e: Superconductors expel magnetic fields from their interior. This perfectly diamagnetic behavior allows them to stably levitate above permanent magnets, a principle used in Maglev trains.
lab.p12thermomechanics_to_levitate_a_tra: To levitate a train of mass 50,000 kg over an area of 50 m², the magnetic pressure P = F/A must equal the pressure from gravity. Given P = B² / 2μ₀ (where μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ T·m/A) and g = 9.8 m/s². Calculate the required Magnetic Field (B) in milliTesla (mT).
lab.p12thermomechanics_unlike_normal_obj: Unlike normal objects, adding mass to a white dwarf makes it
lab.p12thermomechanics_when_a_medium_siz: When a medium-sized star exhausts its nuclear fuel, it collapses under its own gravity to form a White Dwarf.
lab.p9_density_correct_fb: Excellent! The density is {d} g/cm³. Based on this, the material is likely {material}.
lab.p9_density_density_error: Measurements are correct, but your calculated density is wrong. Use ρ = m / (V2 - V1).
lab.p9_density_hint: ρ = m / V. Volume = V2 - V1 (displacement). The cylinder starts at V1=50 mL.
lab.p9_density_measure_error: Your raw measurements (Mass, V1, or V2) are incorrect. Check the balance and cylinder readings carefully.
lab.p9_density_theory: Density is the mass per unit volume of a substance. ρ = m / V. To find the density of an irregular solid: Measure its mass (m) using an electronic balance. Measure initial water volume (V1) in a measuring cylinder. Immerse the solid completely. Measure final water volume (V2). The volume of the solid is V = V2 - V1. Note: 1 mL of water = 1 cm³ of volume.
lab.p9_everyday_concept1: When an object is dropped, gravity accelerates it downwards at roughly 9.8 m/s². The height and time are related by: h = ½gt².
lab.p9_everyday_concept2: A pendulum swings back and forth. The time for one full swing (Period, T) depends entirely on its length and gravity: T = 2π√(L/g).
lab.p9_everyday_concept3: A sliding box slows down because the floor exerts a frictional force. The distance it slides before stopping depends on initial velocity and the coefficient of friction (μ): d = v₀² / (2μg).
lab.p9_everyday_friction_correct: Correct! The coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.5.
lab.p9_everyday_friction_desc: Push the box. Calculate μ using μ = v₀² / (2gd) (use g=9.8)
lab.p9_everyday_friction_incorrect: Incorrect. Use μ = v₀² / (2 * 9.8 * d) using logs.
lab.p9_everyday_gravity_correct: Correct! The acceleration due to gravity is approximately 9.8 m/s².
lab.p9_everyday_gravity_desc: Drop the ball. Calculate g (m/s²) using g = 2h / t²
lab.p9_everyday_gravity_incorrect: Incorrect. Remember to use g = 2h / t² with the measured values.
lab.p9_everyday_instructions: Adjust the shelf height, then click the ball to drop it. Adjust the pendulum length, then click the bob to swing it. Click Push to slide the box across the floor.
lab.p9_everyday_no_data: Interact with objects to log data.
lab.p9_everyday_theory: Physics governs everything in our daily environment! Three key concepts: Free Fall (h = ½gt²), Periodic Motion (T = 2π√(L/g)), and Kinetic Friction (d = v₀² / (2μg)).
lab.p9_friction_assessment_q: Select the "Unknown Material" mode, run the simulation, and record the Peak Force. Calculate its coefficient of static friction (μs). (Assume g = 9.8 m/s²)
lab.p9_friction_hint: F_max = μs × m × g. Kinetic friction is usually lower than static. Try the rolling mode to compare!
lab.p9_friction_incorrect_fb: Incorrect. Use formula: μs = Peak Force / (Mass × 9.8)
lab.p9_friction_instructions: Adjust mass and surface mode, then start pulling to observe static vs kinetic friction. Record your data in the table.
lab.p9_friction_theory: Friction is the force resisting the relative motion of solid surfaces. Static friction must be overcome to start moving. Once moving, a lower kinetic friction applies. Rolling friction is generally much less than sliding friction.
lab.p9_inertia_correct_fb: Great job! The rapid force overcomes static friction quickly, allowing the coin's inertia to keep it over the glass until it falls.
lab.p9_inertia_incorrect_fb: Some answers are incorrect. Review the theory on inertia and friction, and try again.
lab.p9_inertia_q1: Why did the coin drop into the glass when flicked hard?
lab.p9_inertia_q1_a: The card transferred its kinetic energy to the coin.
lab.p9_inertia_q1_b: The coin's inertia resisted sideways motion while gravity pulled it down.
lab.p9_inertia_q1_c: The flicking force pushed the coin directly downward.
lab.p9_inertia_q2: If you slowly pull the card instead of flicking it, what happens?
lab.p9_inertia_q2_a: Static friction is not broken; the coin moves with the card.
lab.p9_inertia_q2_b: The coin will float in the air.
lab.p9_inertia_q2_c: The coin drops into the glass immediately.
lab.p9_inertia_theory: Newton's First Law of Motion (Law of Inertia): An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced force. Inertia is the tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion. The more mass an object has, the more inertia it has.
lab.p9_kinematics_correct_fb: Correct based on your time! (True speed was {v} m/s)
lab.p9_kinematics_hint: Speed = Distance ÷ Time. The pitch is 20.12m. Try measuring the time accurately with the stopwatch!
lab.p9_kinematics_incorrect_fb: Incorrect. Divide distance by YOUR time.
lab.p9_kinematics_sigfig_desc: The red line shows the edge of the textbook. The ruler marks are in centimeters (cm). Record the length to the correct number of significant figures.
lab.p9_kinematics_sigfig_incorrect: Incorrect. Look closely at the marks. Estimate one digit past the smallest mark.
lab.p9_kinematics_theory1: Average speed is the total distance traveled divided by the total time taken. Speed (v) = Distance (d) / Time (t)
lab.p9_kinematics_theory2: When taking a measurement, record all certain digits plus one estimated digit. If a ruler has 1mm marks, you can estimate to 0.1mm (0.01cm).
lab.p9_magfields_assessment_q: Based on your observations, what is the approximate direction (in degrees, 0-360, where 0=right, 90=down) of the magnetic field directly above the center of the magnet?
lab.p9_magfields_correct: Correct! The field points from North to South, which is directly to the left (180°) in the region exactly above the magnet.
lab.p9_magfields_incorrect: Incorrect. Try placing a compass directly above the center of the magnet. Which way does the arrow point? (0°=Right, 90°=Down, 180°=Left)
lab.p9_magfields_instructions:
1. Select a tool below.
2. Compass: Click anywhere around the magnet to see the field direction.
3. Iron Filings: Sprinkle them to reveal the overall field pattern.
lab.p9_magfields_no_data: Place compasses on the simulator to record field directions.
lab.p9_magfields_theory: A magnetic field is the region around a magnet where magnetic forces act. Key properties: They always point from the North pole to the South pole outside the magnet. They never cross each other. They are continuous loops returning through the magnet's interior. The closer the lines, the stronger the field.
lab.p9_magind_assessment_q: If you stroke the unmagnetized steel bar from Left to Right with the North pole of the magnet, which end of the bar becomes the North pole (Left or Right)?
lab.p9_magind_correct: Correct! The domains' North poles are repelled by the magnet's North pole, ending up pointing left. So the left end becomes North.
lab.p9_magind_guess_left: The domains' North poles are repelled by the magnet's North pole, ending up pointing left. So the left end becomes North.
lab.p9_magind_incorrect: Incorrect. Observe the red arrows (North poles of domains) after stroking from left to right. Which direction do they point?
lab.p9_magind_instructions:
1. Drag the magnet repeatedly over the steel bar from one end to the other to observe the domains aligning.
2. Change the bar's orientation and press Hammer Bar to see the effects.
lab.p9_magind_no_data: Interact with the simulator to record data.
lab.p9_magind_theory: A piece of unmagnetized steel contains tiny magnetic domains (groups of atoms) that are randomly oriented. Because they point in all directions, their magnetic fields cancel out. Stroking: Repeatedly dragging a permanent magnet over the steel in one direction forces the domains to align. Hammering: Striking the metal vibrates the domains, allowing them to align with an external field.
lab.p9_measurement_clamp_message: Please clamp the object tightly before measuring.
lab.p9_measurement_correct_fb: Correct! The object is exactly {w} mm wide.
lab.p9_measurement_formula: Total Reading = Main Scale Reading + (Vernier Division × Least Count). Here, Least Count = 0.1 mm
lab.p9_measurement_incorrect_fb: Incorrect. Check the main scale before the vernier zero, then find the matching vernier line.
lab.p9_measurement_theory: A Vernier Caliper is an instrument used to measure internal and external dimensions accurately. Main Scale: Reads in millimeters (mm). Read the mark just to the left of the zero on the Vernier scale. Vernier Scale: Provides precision up to 0.1 mm. Find the Vernier mark that perfectly aligns with any mark on the Main scale.
lab.p9_pressure_assessment_q: Assuming the density of water is 1000 kg/m³ and g = 9.8 m/s², calculate the theoretical fluid pressure (in Pascals) at the lowest hole (depth = 80 cm).
lab.p9_pressure_hint: P = ρgh. Use ρ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.8 m/s². Convert 80cm to 0.8m!
lab.p9_pressure_instructions:
1. Click on the black plugs on the side of the can to let water spurt out.
2. Notice how far the stream travels from the base.
3. Drag the red measuring tool on the ground to precisely measure the distance of the splash point.
4. Record your readings in the Data table.
lab.p9_pressure_theory: Fluid pressure increases with depth due to the weight of the fluid above. The pressure P at depth h in a liquid of density ρ is: P = ρ × g × h. Higher pressure results in a higher ejection velocity (v) from a hole, causing the water to spurt further.
lab.p9_springs_hint: Springs in series have the same tension. The total extension is the sum of each spring's extension. 1/k_eq = 1/k1 + 1/k2 + ...
lab.p9_springs_instructions: Adjust the number of springs and hanging mass. Observe how the force reading is the same on each spring while the total extension adds up.
lab.p9_springs_q1: 1. If 4 identical ideal springs hold a 200g mass, what does the top spring read (in N)?
lab.p9_springs_q2: 2. What is the equivalent spring constant (N/m) of 2 springs in series if a single spring has k=100 N/m?
lab.p9_springs_theory: When ideal (massless) spring balances are connected in series, the tension is the same throughout the chain. Thus, every spring reads the same force. However, the total extension of the system is the sum of the extensions of each individual spring.

lab.recoil_adjust_desc: Adjust sliders, then press FIRE! to see conservation of momentum at work.
lab.recoil_correct_feedback: Correct! vg = 1.5 m/s. The rifle kicks back at just 1.5 m/s because it's much heavier than the bullet.
lab.recoil_final_desc: The gun recoils opposite to the bullet's direction
lab.recoil_incorrect_feedback: Not quite. Use vg = (mb × vb) / mg = (0.012 × 500) / 4
lab.recoil_practice_q: A 4 kg rifle fires a 12 g bullet at 500 m/s.
lab.recoil_practice_q2: What is the recoil velocity of the rifle?
lab.recoil_real_life: Firefighters must brace their footing before using a hose — the water shooting forward creates equal backward force (recoil), just like a rocket expelling gas to move in space.
lab.recoil_step1_detail: 🎯 You're at a shooting range, a 4 kg rifle resting on your shoulder. You breathe, aim at the target, and squeeze the trigger. The gun + bullet form an "isolated system" — all the momentum before firing must equal all the momentum after.
lab.recoil_step2_detail: Before firing, the rifle and the 10 g bullet are both completely still in your hands. Total initial momentum = 0. Everything is quiet, steady — zero motion, zero momentum. Then the firing pin strikes.
lab.recoil_step3_detail: BANG! The bullet rockets forward at 400 m/s. Its momentum: 0.01 × 400 = 4 kg·m/s forward. By conservation, the gun must have −4 kg·m/s momentum backward into your shoulder: m_b·v_b + m_g·v_g = 0.
lab.recoil_step4_detail: Solving: v_g = −(m_b·v_b)/m_g = −(0.01 × 400)/4 = −1 m/s. The negative sign? The gun kicks backward into your shoulder at 1 m/s. A heavier gun recoils less — that's why competition shooters use massive rifles. Rockets work the same way: exhaust shoots down, the rocket goes up!
lab.recoil_subtitle: Follow how conservation of momentum gives the recoil formula
lab.recoil_title: Derivation: Recoil Velocity of a Gun

lab.step1_detail: 🚴 You're cycling at 10 m/s down a neighborhood street when a child suddenly runs out chasing a ball. You slam the brakes. Newton's 2nd law says F = ma — the brake pads squeeze the rim, applying a force that decelerates you.
lab.step2_detail: Your speed drops from 10 m/s to 0 in just 2 seconds. That rate of change is acceleration: a = (0 − 10)/2 = −5 m/s². Your body lurches forward — that's inertia fighting the change. The negative sign? You're slowing down, which means the force is backward.
lab.step3_detail: You + your bike = 80 kg. F = 80 × (0 − 10)/2 = −400 N. Distribute the mass: F = (80×0 − 80×10)/2 = (0 − 800)/2. Your brakes are applying 400 N of force — about the weight of a small adult pushing against your motion!
lab.step4_detail: Momentum p = mv captures your "quantity of motion." You started with p = 80 × 10 = 800 kg·m/s. Over 2 seconds, that momentum drops to zero. So F = Δp/Δt = (0 − 800)/2 = −400 N. Force is simply how fast momentum changes. This is why airbags save lives — they increase impact time Δt, which reduces the force F!

sin θ = opposite / hypotenuse = a/c. cos: sin θ = opposite / hypotenuse = a/c. cos θ = adjacent / hypotenuse = b/c. tan θ = opposite / adjacent = a/b. These three ratios define any right triangle.
sin(2alpha) = 2 sin alpha cos alpha, cos: sin(2alpha) = 2 sin alpha cos alpha, cos(2alpha) = cos^2 alpha - sin^2 alpha
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 — it doesn't matter wh: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 — it doesn't matter what θ is, this always holds true! From this, we can derive: sec²θ − tan²θ = 1 and cosec²θ − cot²θ = 1. These are the Pythagorean Identities, the most fundamental relationships in trigonometry. They're used in everything from signal processing to 3D graphics to orbital mechanics!
such an ordeal requires resilience. By t: such an ordeal requires resilience. By this time tomorrow, they
tan theta = \\frac{sin theta}{cos theta}: tan theta = \frac{sin theta}{cos theta}, \quad \cot theta = \frac{cos theta}{sin theta}
text{Altitudes AD, BE, CF are concurrent: text{Altitudes AD, BE, CF are concurrent}
text{Medians AD, BE, CF are concurrent a: text{Medians AD, BE, CF are concurrent at centroid}
text{Right bisectors of sides are concur: text{Right bisectors of sides are concurrent}
the coordinates when the system crashed.
theorem.class10.alternate_segment.finalF: Angle between tangent and chord equals angle in the alternate segment.
theorem.class10.alternate_segment.keyIns: Sailors use this theorem: the angle between a coastline (tangent) and a line to a lighthouse (chord) determines their position. It's one of the most beautiful theorems in circle geometry!
theorem.class10.alternate_segment.step1_: 🎯 Draw a circle with tangent PT at point A. Draw chord AB, creating ∠APT between tangent and chord.
theorem.class10.alternate_segment.step2_: The chord AB divides the circle into two segments. The alternate segment is opposite the tangent.
theorem.class10.alternate_segment.step3_: The angle between tangent and chord equals the angle in the alternate segment.
theorem.class10.alternate_segment.title: Theorem 10.6: Alternate Segment Theorem
theorem.class10.area_aas.finalFormulaDes: The area using one side and two angles.
theorem.class10.area_aas.keyInsight: Perfect for measuring mountains! When you can't measure both adjacent sides but can measure angles from a distance, this formula gives you the area.
theorem.class10.area_aas.practice_errorH: Δ = ½ a² sin β sin γ / sin α
theorem.class10.area_aas.practice_questi: a=7, β=70°, γ=60°. α=50°. Find the area.
theorem.class10.area_aas.step1_detail: 📋 When you know two angles (β, γ) and the included side a (AAS), start from Δ = ½ ab sin γ.
theorem.class10.area_aas.step2_detail: From Law of Sines: b/sin β = a/sin α → b = a sin β / sin α.
theorem.class10.area_aas.step2_label: Use Law of Sines to find b
theorem.class10.area_aas.step3_detail: Δ = ½ a × (a sin β / sin α) × sin γ. This formula requires only one side length and two angles!
theorem.class10.area_aas.title: Area of a Triangle (AAS)
theorem.class10.area_sas.finalFormulaDes: The area of a triangle using two sides and the included angle.
theorem.class10.area_sas.keyInsight: Land surveyors love this formula: measure two sides of a plot and the angle between them, and you get the area instantly — no need to walk the entire perimeter!
theorem.class10.area_sas.practice_questi: b=5, c=6, α=30°. Find the area.
theorem.class10.area_sas.step1_detail: 🌳 Take side c = AB as the base. The height is the perpendicular from C to AB.
theorem.class10.area_sas.step2_detail: From right triangle ACD, the height h = b sin α.
theorem.class10.area_sas.step3_detail: If α = 90°, sin 90° = 1, so Δ = ½bc — the familiar right triangle area formula!
theorem.class10.area_sas.title: Area of a Triangle (SAS)
theorem.class10.central_angle.finalFormu: The central angle is double any inscribed angle subtended by the same arc.
theorem.class10.central_angle.keyInsight: This explains why the angle in a semicircle is 90° — the central angle is 180° (a straight line), so the inscribed angle is half: 90°. This is the "Golden Theorem" of circle geometry!
theorem.class10.central_angle.practice_q: An inscribed angle subtended by an arc is 35°. What is the central angle?
theorem.class10.central_angle.step1_deta: 📐 Draw chord CD. Central angle ∠COD and inscribed angle ∠CED subtend the same chord CD.
theorem.class10.central_angle.step2_deta: Extend OE to F. Since OC = OE (radii), triangle COE is isosceles. The exterior angle ∠COF = 2∠CEO.
theorem.class10.central_angle.step2_labe: Join O to E and extend
theorem.class10.central_angle.step4_deta: ∠COD = 2∠CEO + 2∠DEO = 2(∠CEO + ∠DEO) = 2∠CED. The central angle is ALWAYS twice the inscribed angle!
theorem.class10.central_angle.title: Theorem 10.7: Central Angle = 2 × Inscribed Angle
theorem.class10.centre_bisects.finalForm: A line from the centre to the midpoint of a chord is perpendicular to the chord.
theorem.class10.centre_bisects.keyInsigh: This is why a spirit level works — the bubble's centre line is perpendicular to the surface. In construction, this theorem ensures circular pipes are level.
theorem.class10.centre_bisects.practice_: In a circle of radius 10, a chord has midpoint 6 units from centre. Find half the chord length.
theorem.class10.centre_bisects.step1_det: ⚽ Take a chord AB on a circle. Mark its midpoint C. Draw a line from the centre O to C.
theorem.class10.centre_bisects.step1_lab: Draw chord AB with midpoint C
theorem.class10.centre_bisects.step2_det: OA and OB are radii, so OA = OB. Triangle OAB is isosceles with apex at O.
theorem.class10.centre_bisects.step3_det: In isosceles triangle OAB, the line from O to the midpoint C of base AB is also the altitude. So OC ⟂ AB.
theorem.class10.centre_bisects.title: Theorem 9.2: Centre to Midpoint of Chord is Perpendicular
theorem.class10.circum_radius.finalFormu: The radius of the circle passing through all three vertices.
theorem.class10.circum_radius.keyInsight: The circumradius relates the three sides and the area! For an equilateral triangle, R = a/√3. The larger the triangle, the larger the circumcircle. This is used in gear and wheel design.
theorem.class10.circum_radius.step1_deta: 🌍 Draw a circle through all three vertices. Draw diameter BD. Since α and ∠BDC are in the same segment, ∠BDC = α.
theorem.class10.circum_radius.step2_deta: In right triangle BCD (angle in a semicircle): a/(2R) = sin α → R = a/(2 sin α).
theorem.class10.circum_radius.step3_deta: From area formula: Δ = ½ bc sin α → sin α = 2Δ/(bc).
theorem.class10.circum_radius.step3_labe: Express in terms of sides and area
theorem.class10.circum_radius.step4_deta: R = a/(2 × 2Δ/(bc)) = abc/(4Δ). The circumradius relates all three sides and the area!
theorem.class10.circum_radius.title: Circum-Radius of a Triangle
theorem.class10.congruent_arcs.keyInsigh: Equal gear teeth (arcs) must have equal chord lengths for smooth meshing. In map-making, equal arcs of latitude create equal chords on the globe.
theorem.class10.congruent_arcs.practice_: Yes, congruent arcs have equal chords.
Equal central angles → equal arcs → equal chords.
Arcs AB and CD each subtend 60° at the centre. Are chords AB and CD equal?
theorem.class10.congruent_arcs.step1_det: 🎵 Imagine two identical arcs on a circle — like equal-length curved sections of a circular railway track.
theorem.class10.congruent_arcs.step2_det: Since the arcs are congruent, rotate one arc onto the other. Their endpoints match up.
theorem.class10.congruent_arcs.step3_det: The chords connect corresponding endpoints. Since arcs perfectly overlap, chords must be equal.
theorem.class10.congruent_arcs.title: Theorem 9.6: Congruent Arcs → Equal Chords
theorem.class10.cyclic_quad.finalFormula: The sum of opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral is 180°.
theorem.class10.cyclic_quad.keyInsight: A rectangular picture frame can always be inscribed in a circle because its opposite angles are 90°+90°=180°. This theorem is used in surveying to check if four points lie on a circle!
theorem.class10.cyclic_quad.step1_detail: 🔷 Draw a quadrilateral ABCD inscribed in a circle (all vertices on the circle).
theorem.class10.cyclic_quad.step2_detail: Join O to A, B, C, D. Arc BCD subtends reflex ∠BOD at centre. Arc BAD subtends ∠BOD.
theorem.class10.cyclic_quad.step3_detail: ∠A = ½×(reflex ∠BOD) and ∠C = ½×(∠BOD). The two central angles sum to 360°.
theorem.class10.cyclic_quad.step4_detail: ∠A + ∠C = ½(360°) = 180°. Similarly, ∠B + ∠D = 180°. Opposite angles of a cyclic quadrilateral are supplementary!
theorem.class10.cyclic_quad.title: Theorem 10.12: Opposite Angles of Cyclic Quadrilateral are Supplementary
theorem.class10.equal_angles_chords.fina: Equal angles at the centre subtend equal chords.
theorem.class10.equal_angles_chords.keyI: This completes the cycle: equal chords ↔ equal central angles ↔ congruent arcs. Used in engineering to create evenly spaced holes on a circular flange!
theorem.class10.equal_angles_chords.prac: Yes, equal central angles always subtend equal chords.
Central angles ∠AOB = ∠COD = 45°. Are chords AB and CD equal?
theorem.class10.equal_angles_chords.step: 📐 Draw two chords AB and CD with equal central angles.
Draw chords with equal central angles
OA = OC (radii), OB = OD (radii), ∠AOB = ∠COD (given). SAS congruence.
From the congruence, AB = CD. Equal central angles subtend equal chords.
theorem.class10.equal_angles_chords.titl: Theorem 9.9: Equal Central Angles → Equal Chords
theorem.class10.equal_chords_angles.fina: Equal chords subtend equal angles at the centre.
theorem.class10.equal_chords_angles.keyI: Equal pizza crust slices (chords) mean equal central angles — giving you equal-sized slices! This theorem is why equal spacing on a circle creates equal angles, fundamental in polygon construction.
theorem.class10.equal_chords_angles.prac: Equal chords subtend equal central angles
∠COD = 60°. Equal chords subtend equal angles at the centre.
Chord AB = chord CD in the same circle. If ∠AOB = 60°, what is ∠COD?
theorem.class10.equal_chords_angles.step: 🎯 Draw two equal chords AB and CD in a circle. Connect their endpoints to the centre O.
OA = OC (radii), OB = OD (radii), AB = CD (given). SSS congruence.
From the congruence, ∠AOB = ∠COD. Equal chords subtend equal central angles.
theorem.class10.equal_chords_angles.titl: Theorem 9.8: Equal Chords → Equal Angles at Centre
theorem.class10.equal_chords_arcs.keyIns: Equal chords ↔ equal central angles ↔ congruent arcs. This one-to-one correspondence is fundamental to circular geometry and is used in compass and straightedge constructions.
theorem.class10.equal_chords_arcs.practi: Equal chords always subtend congruent arcs.
Equal chords → equal central angles → equal arcs.
Two equal chords subtend what kind of arcs?
theorem.class10.equal_chords_arcs.step1_: 🔗 Draw two equal chords AB and CD on a circle, like two equal-length strings on a circular instrument.
theorem.class10.equal_chords_arcs.step2_: OA = OB = OC = OD = r and AB = CD. So triangles are congruent by SSS.
theorem.class10.equal_chords_arcs.step3_: From congruency of triangles, the central angles are equal. Equal central angles mean equal arcs.
theorem.class10.equal_chords_arcs.title: Theorem 9.7: Equal Chords → Congruent Arcs
Equal chords are at the same distance from the centre.
Two guitar strings (chords) of the same length are the same distance from the centre of the circular sound hole. This is used in engineering to balance forces in circular structures.
Equal chords are equidistant from centre
Equal chords are equidistant from the centre.
Equal chords → equal distances from centre.
Two equal chords of length 16 have distance 6 from centre. What is the distance?
✏️ Draw two chords AB and CD of equal length. Draw perpendiculars OE and OF from the centre to each chord.
By Theorem 9.3, E and F are midpoints. AE = AB/2, CF = CD/2. Since AB = CD, AE = CF.
In right triangles OAE and OCF, since OA = OC (radii) and AE = CF: OE² = OF² → OE = OF.
Theorem 9.4: Equal Chords are Equidistant from Centre
theorem.class10.equal_tangents.finalForm: The lengths of two tangents from an external point to a circle are equal.
theorem.class10.equal_tangents.keyInsigh: When you draw two tangents from a point to a circle, they're always the same length. This is used in designing V-belt drives around pulleys — both sides of the belt are equal!
theorem.class10.equal_tangents.practice_: Tangents from same point are equal
Both tangents from A are equal: AB = AC = 12 cm.
Two tangents from the same external point are equal.
Two tangents are drawn from point A to a circle. If one tangent is 12 cm, how long is the other?
theorem.class10.equal_tangents.step1_det: 📍 Take point A outside the circle. Draw two tangents AB and AC touching the circle at B and C.
theorem.class10.equal_tangents.step2_det: Join OA, OB, OC. By Theorem 10.2: OB ⟂ AB and OC ⟂ AC, so ∠OBA = ∠OCA = 90°.
theorem.class10.equal_tangents.step3_det: OB = OC (radii), OA = OA (common), ∠OBA = ∠OCA = 90°. RHS congruence → AB = AC.
theorem.class10.equal_tangents.title: Theorem 10.3: Two Tangents from External Point are Equal
theorem.class10.equidistant_chords.final: Chords at equal distance from the centre are equal in length.
theorem.class10.equidistant_chords.keyIn: This completes the "if and only if": chords are equal ↔ equidistant. This symmetry is fundamental in circular grid design and radial structures like Ferris wheels.
theorem.class10.equidistant_chords.pract: Equal distance from centre → equal chords
Yes, chords at equal distance from the centre are always equal in length.
Equal distances from centre → equal chords.
Two chords are both 8 units from the centre of a circle of radius 10. Are they equal?
theorem.class10.equidistant_chords.step1: 🎪 Draw two chords AB and CD such that their perpendicular distances from the centre (OE and OF) are equal.
theorem.class10.equidistant_chords.step2: Since OA = OC (radii) and OE = OF: AE² = CF² → AE = CF.
theorem.class10.equidistant_chords.step3: E and F are midpoints (Theorem 9.3), so AB = 2×AE and CD = 2×CF. Since AE = CF, we get AB = CD.
theorem.class10.equidistant_chords.title: Theorem 9.5: Equidistant Chords are Congruent
theorem.class10.ex_radius.finalFormulaDe: The radius of the excircle opposite vertex A.
theorem.class10.ex_radius.keyInsight: Exradii are always larger than the inradius. Each ex-circle touches one side of the triangle and the extensions of the other two sides. Used in advanced geometry and triangle geometry problems.
theorem.class10.ex_radius.practice_expla: r₁ ≈ 3.68. Compare with inradius r ≈ 2.10 — exradius is larger!
theorem.class10.ex_radius.step1_detail: 🔵 Draw the ex-centre I₁ opposite vertex A. Connect it to all three vertices, creating three regions.
theorem.class10.ex_radius.step2_detail: Δ = Area(I₁AB) + Area(I₁AC) − Area(I₁BC) because I₁BC is outside the original triangle.
theorem.class10.ex_radius.step3_detail: b + c − a = 2S − 2a = 2(S−a). So Δ = ½r₁ × 2(S−a) = r₁(S−a).
theorem.class10.ex_radius.title: Ex-Radius of a Triangle
theorem.class10.external_touching.finalF: If circles touch externally, distance between centres equals sum of radii.
theorem.class10.external_touching.keyIns: When two soap bubbles merge, their centres are separated by the sum of their radii. This is also why ball bearings sit perfectly between inner and outer races.
theorem.class10.external_touching.practi: Two circles of radii 3 cm and 4 cm touch externally. Distance between centres?
theorem.class10.external_touching.step1_: 🔵🔵 Draw two circles with centres A and B that touch at exactly one point C.
theorem.class10.external_touching.step2_: The shortest distance between A and B passes through point of contact C.
Point C lies on line AB
theorem.class10.external_touching.step3_: AC is radius r₁, BC is radius r₂. Since C lies on line AB: AB = r₁ + r₂.
theorem.class10.external_touching.title: Theorem 10.4: Externally Touching Circles
theorem.class10.half_angle_cos.finalForm: Express half-angle cosines directly in terms of side lengths.
theorem.class10.half_angle_cos.keyInsigh: These formulas allow you to compute trigonometric functions of half-angles without needing a calculator — just the side lengths! They're used in lunar and planetary calculations.
theorem.class10.half_angle_cos.practice_: a=5, b=6, c=7. S=9. Find cos(α/2).
theorem.class10.half_angle_cos.step1_det: 📊 cos α = (b²+c²−a²)/(2bc). This gives cos of the full angle in terms of side lengths.
theorem.class10.half_angle_cos.step3_det: After algebra using the semi-perimeter S = (a+b+c)/2, we get cos²(α/2) = S(S−a)/(bc).
theorem.class10.half_angle_cos.step4_det: Taking the positive square root gives the half-angle cosine. This formula expresses a trigonometric ratio purely in terms of side lengths!
theorem.class10.half_angle_cos.title: Half Angle Formula for Cosines
theorem.class10.half_angle_sin.finalForm: Express half-angle sines directly in terms of side lengths.
theorem.class10.half_angle_sin.keyInsigh: Together with the cosine half-angle formula, these form the basis for Hero's formula and are used extensively in navigation and astronomy for celestial calculations!
theorem.class10.half_angle_sin.practice_: a=5, b=6, c=7. S=9. Find sin(α/2).
theorem.class10.half_angle_sin.step1_det: 🔄 Starting with cos α = 1 − 2sin²(α/2), substitute the Law of Cosines.
theorem.class10.half_angle_sin.step2_det: After algebraic manipulation using the semi-perimeter S, we get sin²(α/2) = (S−b)(S−c)/(bc).
