theorem.class10.half_angle_sin.step3_det: Together with the cosine half-angle formula, these are used extensively in navigation and astronomy for celestial calculations!
theorem.class10.half_angle_sin.title: Half Angle Formula for Sines
theorem.class10.heros_formula.finalFormu: The area of a triangle from its three side lengths alone.
theorem.class10.heros_formula.keyInsight: This 2,000-year-old formula by Hero of Alexandria requires ONLY side lengths — no angles, no trigonometry! It's used today in land surveying, GPS area calculation, and even computer graphics.
theorem.class10.heros_formula.practice_e: Δ = √900 = 30. This is a 5-12-13 right triangle!
theorem.class10.heros_formula.practice_q: a=5, b=12, c=13. Find S and the area.
theorem.class10.heros_formula.step1_deta: 📏 You've measured all three sides of a triangular garden: a=5m, b=6m, c=7m. Start with Δ = ½ bc sin α.
theorem.class10.heros_formula.step1_labe: Start with Δ = ½ bc sin α
theorem.class10.heros_formula.step2_deta: Using the double-angle identity: sin α = 2 sin(α/2) cos(α/2). Now substitute the half-angle formulas.
theorem.class10.heros_formula.step3_deta: Substituting sin(α/2) = √[(S−b)(S−c)/(bc)] and cos(α/2) = √[S(S−a)/(bc)], the bc factors cancel, leaving Hero's formula!
theorem.class10.heros_formula.title: Hero's Formula (SSS)
theorem.class10.in_radius.finalFormulaDe: The radius of the circle inscribed inside a triangle.
theorem.class10.in_radius.keyInsight: The incircle is the largest circle that fits inside a triangle. This formula is used to find the radius of a pizza that fits exactly inside a triangular box — the inradius!
theorem.class10.in_radius.practice_expla: r = 2. The incircle of a 6-8-10 right triangle has radius 2.
theorem.class10.in_radius.practice_quest: a=6, b=8, c=10. Area=24. Find the inradius.
theorem.class10.in_radius.step1_detail: 🎯 The incircle touches all three sides of a triangle from the inside. Its centre I is the intersection of the angle bisectors.
theorem.class10.in_radius.step2_detail: The incentre I divides ΔABC into three smaller triangles, each with height r.
theorem.class10.in_radius.step3_detail: Δ = r(a+b+c)/2 = rS. So r = Δ/S.
theorem.class10.in_radius.title: In-Radius of a Triangle
theorem.class10.internal_touching.finalF: If circles touch internally, distance equals difference of radii.
theorem.class10.internal_touching.keyIns: Ball bearings use this principle: the inner race (small circle) touches the outer race (large circle) internally at exactly one point.
theorem.class10.internal_touching.practi: Two circles of radii 5 cm and 2 cm touch internally. Distance between centres?
theorem.class10.internal_touching.step1_: ⭕🔵 Draw a larger circle (radius r₁) and a smaller one (radius r₂) inside it, touching at C.
theorem.class10.internal_touching.step2_: The circles touch at C, which lies on line AB. The smaller circle is inside the larger one.
theorem.class10.internal_touching.step3_: For the outer circle: AC = r₁. For the inner circle: BC = r₂. Since B is inside: AB = r₁ − r₂.
theorem.class10.internal_touching.title: Theorem 10.5: Internally Touching Circles
theorem.class10.law_cosines.finalFormula: Generalizes Pythagoras for ANY triangle, not just right-angled ones.
theorem.class10.law_cosines.keyInsight: Surveyors use this to measure land plots. If you know two sides of a field and the angle between them (SAS), you can calculate the third side without having to walk across it!
theorem.class10.law_cosines.practice_que: b=5, c=6, α=60°. Find side a.
theorem.class10.law_cosines.step1_detail: 🏔️ Drop a perpendicular AD from A to side BC, creating two right triangles.
theorem.class10.law_cosines.step2_detail: CD = a − c cos β. The altitude splits the base into two segments.
theorem.class10.law_cosines.step3_detail: b² = c² sin²β + a² + c² cos²β − 2ac cos β.
theorem.class10.law_cosines.step3_label: Apply Pythagoras in right triangle ADC
theorem.class10.law_cosines.step4_detail: b² = a² + c²(sin²β + cos²β) − 2ac cos β = a² + c² − 2ac cos β. When β = 90°, this becomes the Pythagorean Theorem!
theorem.class10.law_cosines.title: Law of Cosines
theorem.class10.law_sines.finalFormulaDe: The ratio of a side to the sine of its opposite angle is constant.
theorem.class10.law_sines.keyInsight: GPS satellites use this! By measuring angles to multiple satellites, your phone uses the Law of Sines to triangulate your exact position anywhere on Earth.
theorem.class10.law_sines.practice_quest: a=7, α=50°, β=70°. Find side b.
theorem.class10.law_sines.step1_detail: 🌄 Drop a perpendicular from B to base AC. The altitude h can be expressed in two ways: h = c sin α and h = a sin γ.
theorem.class10.law_sines.step1_label: Draw altitude h from B to AC
theorem.class10.law_sines.step2_detail: Since both equal the same altitude: c sin α = a sin γ → a/sin α = c/sin γ.
theorem.class10.law_sines.step3_detail: Repeat with other altitudes to get the complete Law of Sines.
theorem.class10.law_sines.title: Law of Sines
theorem.class10.law_tangents.finalFormul: Relates side lengths to tangent of half angle sums and differences.
theorem.class10.law_tangents.keyInsight: In navigation and surveying, the Law of Tangents is preferred over the Law of Cosines when two sides and the included angle are known, as it avoids square roots and gives cleaner results!
theorem.class10.law_tangents.step1_detai: 📐 From a/sin α = b/sin β, we get a/b = sin α/sin β.
theorem.class10.law_tangents.step2_detai: Using componendo-dividendo: (a+b)/(a−b) = (sin α + sin β)/(sin α − sin β).
theorem.class10.law_tangents.step3_detai: sin α + sin β = 2 sin[(α+β)/2] cos[(α−β)/2] and sin α − sin β = 2 cos[(α+β)/2] sin[(α−β)/2]. The ratio simplifies to the tangent form.
theorem.class10.law_tangents.title: Law of Tangents
theorem.class10.major_segment.finalFormu: An angle in a segment greater than a semicircle is acute.
theorem.class10.major_segment.keyInsight: Combined with Theorems 10.11, this gives a complete classification of angles in circular segments. The semicircle at 90° is the boundary between acute and obtuse.
theorem.class10.major_segment.practice_e: Angles in a major segment are acute (< 90°).
theorem.class10.major_segment.practice_q: An angle in a major segment is: acute or obtuse?
theorem.class10.major_segment.step1_deta: 📐 Draw chord AB creating a major segment (larger than a semicircle). Pick a point P on the major arc.
theorem.class10.major_segment.step1_labe: Draw chord AB forming a major segment
theorem.class10.major_segment.step2_deta: The central angle subtended by the major segment is less than 180°.
theorem.class10.major_segment.step3_deta: Since the central angle is less than 180°, the inscribed angle is less than 90° (acute).
theorem.class10.major_segment.title: Theorem 10.10: Angle in Major Segment < 90°
theorem.class10.minor_segment.finalFormu: An angle in a segment less than a semicircle is obtuse.
theorem.class10.minor_segment.keyInsight: This completes the classification: major segment → acute (< 90°), semicircle → right (= 90°), minor segment → obtuse (> 90°). This beautiful result explains why right angles are so special!
theorem.class10.minor_segment.practice_e: Angles in a minor segment are obtuse (> 90°).
theorem.class10.minor_segment.practice_q: An angle in a minor segment is: acute or obtuse?
theorem.class10.minor_segment.step1_deta: 📐 Draw chord AB forming a minor segment (smaller than a semicircle).
theorem.class10.minor_segment.step1_labe: Draw chord forming a minor segment
theorem.class10.minor_segment.step2_deta: The central angle subtended by the minor segment is greater than 180°.
theorem.class10.minor_segment.step3_deta: Half of an angle greater than 180° gives an inscribed angle greater than 90°.
theorem.class10.minor_segment.title: Theorem 10.11: Angle in Minor Segment > 90°
theorem.class10.perp_bisects.finalFormul: The perpendicular from the centre to a chord bisects it.
theorem.class10.perp_bisects.keyInsight: This is the converse of Theorem 9.2. Together, they form the foundation of all chord calculations — used in designing arches, bridges, and circular structures like the Colosseum!
theorem.class10.perp_bisects.practice_qu: A chord 16 units long has distance 6 from the centre. Find the radius.
theorem.class10.perp_bisects.step1_detai: 🎯 Draw a chord AB and a perpendicular from centre O to meet AB at C.
theorem.class10.perp_bisects.step2_detai: OA and OB are radii, so OA = OB. Triangle OAB is isosceles.
theorem.class10.perp_bisects.step3_detai: OA = OB (radii), OC = OC (common), ∠OCA = ∠OCB = 90°. So triangles are congruent by RHS. Therefore AC = BC.
theorem.class10.perp_bisects.title: Theorem 9.3: Perpendicular from Centre Bisects Chord
theorem.class10.perp_radius_tangent.fina: A line perpendicular to a radius at its endpoint on the circle is a tangent.
theorem.class10.perp_radius_tangent.keyI: This is the construction method for tangents — simply draw a perpendicular at the endpoint of a radius. Used in designing roller coaster tracks and railway curves!
theorem.class10.perp_radius_tangent.prac: Perpendicular at endpoint of radius = tangent
It's a tangent to the circle.
A line perpendicular to radius at endpoint is a...
A line is drawn perpendicular to a radius at its endpoint on the circle. What is it?
theorem.class10.perp_radius_tangent.step: 🔄 Draw a radial segment OA from centre O to point A on the circle. Draw line PQ ⟂ OA at A.
Since OA is the perpendicular distance, it's the shortest distance from O to PQ. Any other point on PQ is farther from O than OA.
The distance from O to any other point on PQ is greater than the radius, so no other point lies on the circle.
A line touching a circle at exactly one point is a tangent. Draw a perpendicular at a radius endpoint to construct a tangent!
theorem.class10.quadratic_formula.finalF: A universal formula to solve any quadratic equation ax² + bx + c = 0.
theorem.class10.quadratic_formula.keyIns: The discriminant D = b²−4ac tells you everything: D > 0 means 2 real roots (parabola crosses x-axis twice), D = 0 means 1 root (touches once), D < 0 means no real roots (never crosses). Architects use this to design arches that perfectly span a given distance!
theorem.class10.quadratic_formula.practi: x = 2 or 3. The quadratic formula finds both roots!
theorem.class10.quadratic_formula.step1_: 🏗️ You're an architect designing a parabolic arch. The curve follows ax² + bx + c = 0. You need a formula that works for ANY arch shape. Start with the general quadratic equation.
theorem.class10.quadratic_formula.step2_: Shift the constant c to the right side: ax² + bx = −c. The arch's vertical offset doesn't affect its shape — it just shifts it up or down.
theorem.class10.quadratic_formula.step3_: Divide by a to make the coefficient of x² equal to 1. This sets up the next step: completing the square.
theorem.class10.quadratic_formula.step4_: Add (b/2a)² to both sides: x² + (b/a)x + (b/2a)² = (b/2a)² − c/a. The left simplifies to (x + b/2a)², and the right becomes (b²−4ac)/(4a²).
theorem.class10.quadratic_formula.step5_: Take the square root: x + b/2a = ±√(b²−4ac)/(2a). Isolate x to get the Quadratic Formula. This is the single most used formula in algebra!
theorem.class10.quadratic_formula.title: Derivation of the Quadratic Formula
theorem.class10.same_segment.finalFormul: All angles subtended by a chord in the same segment are equal.
theorem.class10.same_segment.keyInsight: Any point on a circular arc "sees" a chord at the same angle. This is the principle behind the "goal line" in circular sports stadiums — every seat has the same viewing angle!
theorem.class10.same_segment.practice_er: Angles in same segment are equal
theorem.class10.same_segment.practice_ex: ∠ADB = 40° since both are in the same segment.
theorem.class10.same_segment.practice_hi: Angles in the same segment are equal.
theorem.class10.same_segment.practice_qu: ∠ACB = 40° in a segment. What is ∠ADB if D is also in the same segment?
theorem.class10.same_segment.step1_detai: 🎯 Draw a chord AB. Take three points C, D, E on the SAME segment of the circle.
theorem.class10.same_segment.step1_label: Take chord AB and points on same segment
theorem.class10.same_segment.step2_detai: Each of these angles is subtended by chord AB at different points on the same arc.
theorem.class10.same_segment.step3_detai: Each equals half the central angle ∠AOB (Theorem 10.7). Since they equal half of the same central angle, they must be equal!
theorem.class10.same_segment.title: Theorem 10.8: Angles in Same Segment are Equal
theorem.class10.semicircle.finalFormulaD: An angle inscribed in a semicircle is always 90°.
theorem.class10.semicircle.keyInsight: Attributed to Thales of Miletus (c. 600 BCE), this is one of the oldest known theorems. Egyptian pyramid builders used it to construct right angles. It's the basis for the "circle of possibilities" in GPS triangulation!
theorem.class10.semicircle.practice_hint: Angle in a semicircle is always...
theorem.class10.semicircle.practice_ques: AB is a diameter. Point P is on the semicircle. What is ∠APB?
theorem.class10.semicircle.step1_detail: 🌓 Draw a circle with diameter AB passing through centre O. Take any point P on the semicircle arc.
theorem.class10.semicircle.step2_detail: The central angle for chord AB is ∠AOB = 180°. By Theorem 10.7, ∠APB = ½ × 180° = 90°.
theorem.class10.semicircle.step3_detail: No matter where P is on the semicircle arc, ∠APB always equals 90°!
theorem.class10.semicircle.title: Theorem 10.9: Angle in a Semicircle is a Right Angle
theorem.class10.tangent_perp_radius.fina: The tangent and the radius at the point of contact are perpendicular.
theorem.class10.tangent_perp_radius.keyI: This is why a bicycle wheel's spokes (radii) are perpendicular to the ground (tangent) at the contact point. This property is fundamental in wheel design and railway engineering.
theorem.class10.tangent_perp_radius.prac: Tangent is perpendicular to radius at point of contact.
Radius OB meets tangent at B. What is the angle between them?
theorem.class10.tangent_perp_radius.step: ⭕ Draw a circle with centre O. Draw a tangent PQ touching the circle at B. Draw radius OB.
If OB is NOT perpendicular, then some other point on PQ is closer to O than B.
That closer point would be inside the circle, meaning PQ intersects the circle at two points — contradicting that PQ is a tangent.
The assumption is false, so OB ⟂ PQ. The radius and tangent are always perpendicular.
theorem.class10.tangent_perp_radius.titl: Theorem 10.2: Tangent ⟂ Radius at Point of Contact
theorem.class10.three_point_circle.final: Three points not on a line uniquely define a circle.
theorem.class10.three_point_circle.keyIn: This theorem is why three legs of a tripod always touch the ground — any three non-collinear points define a unique circle. GPS trilateration uses this principle to locate positions!
theorem.class10.three_point_circle.pract: Any three non-collinear points define one circle
Every triangle (3 non-collinear points) defines exactly one circumcircle.
Every triangle has a unique circumcircle.
Three points form a triangle with sides 3, 4, 5. What shape defines the unique circle through them?
theorem.class10.three_point_circle.step1: 📍 Pick any three points A, B, C that don't lie on a straight line. Connect them to form a triangle.
theorem.class10.three_point_circle.step2: Draw the perpendicular bisectors of any two sides. They meet at a single point O, the circumcentre.
theorem.class10.three_point_circle.step3: O lies on the bisector of AB, so OA = OB. It also lies on the bisector of BC, so OB = OC. Therefore OA = OB = OC.
theorem.class10.three_point_circle.step4: If the points were collinear, the bisectors would be parallel and never meet — proving collinear points can never lie on a circle!
theorem.class10.three_point_circle.title: Theorem 9.1: Three Non-Collinear Points Determine a Circle
theorem.class10.vector_diff.finalFormula: The vector AB equals the position vector of B minus the position vector of A.
theorem.class10.vector_diff.keyInsight: GPS navigation uses this every second! Your phone calculates the vector from your current position to your destination by subtracting their coordinates. This is the foundation of all motion tracking in physics and engineering.
theorem.class10.vector_diff.practice_que: A=(1,2), B=(4,6). Find the x-component of AB.
theorem.class10.vector_diff.step1_detail: 🗺️ You're tracking two delivery drones. Drone A is at (2, 3) and drone B is at (7, 5). Their position vectors from the control tower (origin O) are ã and b⃗.
theorem.class10.vector_diff.step2_detail: To travel from O to B via A: OA + AB = OB. This is the triangle law of vector addition.
theorem.class10.vector_diff.step3_detail: AB = OB − OA. The vector from A to B is the difference between the two position vectors.
theorem.class10.vector_diff.step4_detail: AB = (x₂−x₁, y₂−y₁). The components tell you the displacement in each direction. The magnitude |AB| = √((x₂−x₁)² + (y₂−y₁)²) gives the straight-line distance.
theorem.class10.vector_diff.title: Finding a Vector from Position Vectors
theorem.class11.arithmetico_geometric_su: Sum of an arithmetico-geometric series (arithmetic × geometric).

This series combines arithmetic (adding a constant d each term) with geometric (multiplying by r each term). Used in calculating loan amortization schedules where interest compounds while fixed payments are made!
Write out the first n terms and add them
a=1, d=2, r=3, n=2. Find S₂ (first term = a=1, second term = (a+d)r = 9).
🏦 You're a banker calculating a loan with fixed payments and compound interest. The first payment is a, each subsequent payment increases by d (inflation), and interest compounds at rate r.
🧮 Subtracting: (1−r)Sₙ = a + dr + dr² + ... + drⁿ⁻¹ − (a+nd)rⁿ. The a terms telescope, and the d terms form a geometric series itself!
✅ The middle dr terms form a geometric series: dr(1+r+...+rⁿ⁻²) = dr(1−rⁿ⁻¹)/(1−r). Dividing through by (1−r) gives the complete formula. This is how actuaries calculate tiered annuity payments!
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.finalFo: Expand (a+b)ⁿ as a sum of terms with binomial coefficients.
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.keyInsi: The binomial theorem is the algebraic powerhouse behind probability (binomial distribution), finance (binomial options pricing model), and even fractal geometry (Pascal's Triangle generates the Sierpinski triangle!).
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.practic: Use Pascal's Triangle row n, position k
Row 4 of Pascal's triangle: 1, 4, 6, 4, 1. Coefficient of a²b² = ⁴C₂ = 6
Find the coefficient of a²b² in (a+b)⁴.
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.step1_d: 🌳 You're a geneticist tracking how a trait spreads through a family tree. Each generation inherits one allele from each parent. The expansion of (A+B)ⁿ gives all possible genotype combinations!
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.step2_d: 📐 Assume the theorem holds for n = k: (a+b)ᵏ = Σ ᵏCᵢ aᵏ⁻ⁱ bⁱ.
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.step3_d: 🧮 To prove for k+1, multiply both sides by (a+b): (a+b)ᵏ⁺¹ = (a+b)(ᵏC₀aᵏ + ᵏC₁aᵏ⁻¹b + ... + ᵏCₖbᵏ).
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.step4_d: ✅ After distributing and grouping like terms, the coefficients follow Pascal's identity: ᵏCᵣ₋₁ + ᵏCᵣ = ᵏ⁺¹Cᵣ. Therefore (a+b)ᵏ⁺¹ = Σ ᵏ⁺¹Cᵣ aᵏ⁺¹⁻ʳ bʳ. By induction, the theorem holds for all positive integers n!
theorem.class11.binomial_theorem.title: Binomial Theorem for Positive Integers
theorem.class11.combination_formula.fina: Number of ways to choose r objects from n distinct objects.
theorem.class11.combination_formula.keyI: Combinations count selections without regard to order. If you're picking a 3-person committee from 10 people, the order you pick them doesn't matter — that's ¹⁰C₃ = 120 ways! This formula is the foundation of probability calculations.
theorem.class11.combination_formula.prac: ⁵C₂ = 10. There are 10 ways to choose 2 from 5.
theorem.class11.combination_formula.step: 🎯 You're organizing a 3-person team from 5 volunteers. You first think about permutations (ordered arrangements), but realize order doesn't matter — picking Alice, Bob, Charlie is the same team as Charlie, Bob, Alice.
📐 Each combination of r objects can be arranged in r! different ways. So the number of permutations (ordered) = number of combinations × arrangements: ⁿPᵣ = X × r!.
🧮 We know ⁿPᵣ = n!/(n−r)!. Substituting: X × r! = n!/(n−r)!.

✅ Divide both sides by r!: X = ⁿCᵣ = n!/(r!(n−r)!). For 5-choose-3: ⁵C₃ = 5!/(3!2!) = 120/(6×2) = 10. There are 10 possible 3-person teams from 5 people. This is the foundation of the binomial distribution and Pascal's Triangle!
theorem.class11.complex_division_polar.k: Complex division in polar form is elegant: divide the magnitudes and subtract the angles! This is much simpler than the rectangular form and is used extensively in phasor analysis for AC power systems.
theorem.class11.complex_division_polar.p: r₁/r₂ = 2. The angle of the result is 90°−30° = 60°.
z₁=4(cos 90°+i sin 90°), z₂=2(cos 30°+i sin 30°). Find r₁/r₂.
theorem.class11.complex_division_polar.s: 🔌 An electrical engineer is analyzing a power grid. Two AC voltages are z₁ = r₁(cos θ₁ + i sin θ₁) and z₂ = r₂(cos θ₂ + i sin θ₂). To find their ratio, divide them in polar form.
📐 Multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the denominator.
🧮 After expanding: the denominator becomes cos²θ₂ + sin²θ₂ = 1. The numerator simplifies using angle sum/difference identities.
✅ cos θ₁ cos θ₂ + sin θ₁ sin θ₂ = cos(θ₁−θ₂) and sin θ₁ cos θ₂ − cos θ₁ sin θ₂ = sin(θ₁−θ₂). So z₁/z₂ = (r₁/r₂)[cos(θ₁−θ₂) + i sin(θ₁−θ₂)] — divide magnitudes, subtract angles!
theorem.class11.complex_division_polar.t: Division of Complex Numbers in Polar Form
theorem.class11.complex_inverse_polar.fi: The multiplicative inverse in polar form uses the conjugate.
theorem.class11.complex_inverse_polar.ke: Just like 2⁻¹ = ½ for real numbers, every non-zero complex number z = r(cos θ + i sin θ) has an inverse. The inverse is found by dividing 1 by the magnitude r and negating the angle!
theorem.class11.complex_inverse_polar.st: 🔄 You're designing an electrical circuit with AC signals represented as complex numbers. To find the impedance of a parallel component, you need to compute 1/Z. Starting with z = r(cos θ + i sin θ), write z⁻¹ = 1/z.
📐 To rationalize, multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate cos θ − i sin θ. This is the complex analog of rationalizing a denominator with √.
🧮 Since i² = −1: cos²θ − i² sin²θ = cos²θ + sin²θ = 1 (the Pythagorean identity!). The denominator collapses to r × 1 = r.

✅ z⁻¹ = (1/r)(cos θ − i sin θ). The magnitude is 1/r and the angle is −θ. In AC circuits, this means the inverse impedance has reciprocal magnitude and opposite phase angle — essential for parallel circuit analysis!
theorem.class11.complex_inverse_polar.ti: Inverse of a Complex Number in Polar Form
theorem.class11.complex_magnitude.finalF: The magnitude of z = x + iy is the distance from the origin in the complex plane.
theorem.class11.complex_magnitude.keyIns: The magnitude |z| is also called the modulus or absolute value. It measures how far the complex number z = x + iy is from the origin on the complex plane, just like the distance formula! This is fundamental in signal processing where |z| represents the amplitude of a signal.
theorem.class11.complex_magnitude.practi: |3 + 4i| = 5. The classic 3-4-5 triangle on the complex plane!
theorem.class11.complex_magnitude.step1_: 🗺️ You're a radar operator tracking a plane on a circular radar screen. The plane's position is at (x, y) — the real part x is the east-west coordinate, and the imaginary part y is the north-south coordinate. The distance from the centre (origin) is what matters for range.
theorem.class11.complex_magnitude.step2_: 📏 The magnitude is defined as |z| = √(x² + y²). Since squares of real numbers are always ≥ 0: x² ≥ 0 and y² ≥ 0, so x² + y² ≥ 0. The square root of a non-negative number is also non-negative, therefore |z| ≥ 0.
theorem.class11.complex_magnitude.step3_: 🧮 Suppose |z| = 0. Then √(x² + y²) = 0 → x² + y² = 0. The sum of two squares can only be zero if both are zero: x = 0 AND y = 0. So z = 0 + 0i = 0.
theorem.class11.complex_magnitude.step4_: 🔄 Conversely, if z = 0 = 0 + 0i, then |z| = √(0² + 0²) = 0. Therefore, |z| = 0 if and only if z = 0. This fact is used in control systems to check if a feedback signal has converged to zero!
theorem.class11.complex_magnitude.title: Magnitude of a Complex Number
theorem.class11.determinant_identical.fi: A matrix with two identical rows or columns has determinant zero.
theorem.class11.determinant_identical.ke: This makes intuitive sense: if two rows are the same, the matrix is singular (non-invertible). The system of equations would have redundant information and no unique solution. It's like trying to pin down a point with two identical directions!
theorem.class11.determinant_identical.pr: det(A) = 0. Identical rows make the matrix singular.
Identical rows → the determinant must be zero
A 3×3 matrix has identical first and second rows. What is det(A)?
theorem.class11.determinant_identical.st: 📉 Your GPS receiver uses three satellite signals (rows) to triangulate your position. If two satellites send identical signals (identical rows), the receiver can't distinguish them — it can't find a unique location!
🎯 If we swap Row 1 and Row 2, since they're identical, matrix B is identical to matrix A. But by the interchange property, swapping rows flips the determinant sign: |B| = −|A|.
🧮 Since B = A (identical rows), |B| = |A|. But also |B| = −|A| (from the interchange property). Therefore |A| = −|A|.
✅ Adding |A| to both sides: 2|A| = 0 → |A| = 0. This is why Gauss-Jordan elimination breaks when rows are dependent — the determinant being zero tells us the matrix is singular!
Swapping any two rows or columns multiplies the determinant by −1.

This property reveals that the determinant is an alternating multilinear function. Swapping rows is like flipping the orientation of a parallelogram — the signed area changes sign! Used in computer graphics for polygon winding order detection.
det(A) = 5. You swap two rows. What is the new determinant?
🎯 You're solving a system of 3 equations, and accidentally swapped the first two equations. How does this affect the determinant? Let's swap Row 1 and Row 2 of A to form B.
Swap two rows of A to get B
📐 Expanding |B| by its first row gives a different-looking expression.
🧮 By rearranging terms and factoring, the expression inside brackets matches exactly the expansion of |A| from Row 1.

✅ The determinant changes sign. Swapping rows twice returns the original sign. This property is why Cramer's rule works — and why a determinant with two identical rows must be zero (since swapping them gives |A| = −|A| → 2|A| = 0 → |A| = 0)!
theorem.class11.determinant_transpose.fi: Transposing a matrix does not change its determinant.
theorem.class11.determinant_transpose.ke: The determinant of a matrix and its transpose are always equal. This means row operations and column operations have identical effects on the determinant — a key symmetry that simplifies many proofs in linear algebra!
theorem.class11.determinant_transpose.pr: A 2×2 matrix has det(A) = 7. What is det(Aᵀ)?
theorem.class11.determinant_transpose.st: 📊 You're a cryptographer working with matrix encryption. The matrix A = [[a₁₁, a₁₂, a₁₃], [a₂₁, a₂₂, a₂₃], [a₃₁, a₃₂, a₃₃]] encodes your message. How does the determinant change if you transpose it?
📐 The transpose Aᵀ swaps rows and columns: Aᵀ = [[a₁₁, a₂₁, a₃₁], [a₁₂, a₂₂, a₃₂], [a₁₃, a₂₃, a₃₃]]. The element at position (i,j) in A goes to position (j,i) in Aᵀ.
🧮 Expanding |Aᵀ| from Column 1 gives the exact same expression as expanding |A| from Row 1. Every term matches identically.
✅ The two expansions are identical, so |A| = |Aᵀ|. This is why any theorem about rows in determinants automatically applies to columns too — a powerful symmetry in linear algebra!
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.finalForm: (x−c) is a factor of p(x) if and only if p(c) = 0.
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.keyInsigh: This is the single most powerful tool for factoring polynomials! If you can guess a root (like x=1, x=−1, x=2), you can immediately factor out (x−c) and reduce the polynomial degree by 1. It's like peeling layers off an onion!
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.practice_: Check p(2). If p(2) = 0, it's a factor.
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.step1_det: 🔍 You're a codebreaker trying to factor a complex polynomial. The Factor Theorem gives you a systematic way: if you can find a value c where p(c) = 0, you've found a factor (x−c)!
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.step2_det: 📐 If (x−c) is a factor, the division p(x) ÷ (x−c) leaves no remainder, so r = 0. By the Remainder Theorem, r = p(c). Therefore p(c) = 0.
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.step3_det: 🔄 Conversely, if p(c) = 0, then by the Remainder Theorem the remainder r = 0. Substituting r = 0: p(x) = (x−c)q(x). This proves (x−c) is a factor.
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.step4_det: ✅ For p(x) = x³ − 2x² − x + 2: p(1) = 1 − 2 − 1 + 2 = 0. So (x−1) is a factor! Dividing: p(x) = (x−1)(x² − x − 2) = (x−1)(x−2)(x+1). One root found, the rest follows easily!
theorem.class11.factor_theorem.title: Factor Theorem
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.finalFo: The cosine of a difference equals the product of cosines plus product of sines.
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.keyInsi: This single formula is the mother of all trigonometric identities! From it, we can derive cos(α+β), sin(α±β), double-angle formulas, half-angle formulas, and product-to-sum identities. It's the Rosetta Stone of trigonometry.
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.practic: cos(60°−30°) = cos 60° cos 30° + sin 60° sin 30°. Compute cos(30°).
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.step1_d: 🎯 You're a GPS engineer. The unit circle represents the Earth's equator, and points C and D are two satellites at angles α and β from the prime meridian.
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.step2_d: 📐 The central angle between C and D is α−β. Construct angle AOB = α−β with A(1,0) and B(cos(α−β), sin(α−β)). Since the central angles are equal, chords CD and AB are equal.
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.step3_d: 🧮 Equating CD² = AB² and expanding both sides. On the left: cos²α + cos²β − 2cosαcosβ + sin²α + sin²β − 2sinαsinβ = 2 − 2(cosαcosβ + sinαsinβ). On the right: 1 − 2cos(α−β) + 1 = 2 − 2cos(α−β).
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.step4_d: ✅ Equating: 2 − 2(cosαcosβ + sinαsinβ) = 2 − 2cos(α−β). Cancel 2 and divide by −2: cos(α−β) = cosαcosβ + sinαsinβ. From this single identity flows ALL of trigonometry!
theorem.class11.fundamental_trig.title: Fundamental Law of Trigonometry: cos(α−β)
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.finalFormu: Sum of a geometric series: Sₙ = a₁(1−rⁿ)/(1−r) for r ≠ 1.
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.keyInsight: This formula explains why a ball bouncing to 60% of its height each time travels a finite total distance despite bouncing infinitely — the infinite sum converges! It's also how compound interest calculations work: your bank balance grows geometrically.
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.step1_deta: 💰 You're an investor. You put $1,000 in a fund that grows by 5% each year. Your balance after n years forms a geometric series: year 0: $1000, year 1: $1050, year 2: $1102.50... Each term is the previous × 1.05.
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.step2_deta: 📐 Multiply the entire series by r: rSₙ = a₁r + a₁r² + a₁r³ + ... + a₁rⁿ. This shifts every term by one position.
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.step3_deta: 🧮 Subtract rSₙ from Sₙ: Sₙ − rSₙ = a₁ − a₁rⁿ. All the intermediate terms (a₁r through a₁rⁿ⁻¹) cancel out perfectly — the elegance of the telescoping subtraction!
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.step4_deta: ✅ Sₙ(1−r) = a₁(1−rⁿ) → Sₙ = a₁(1−rⁿ)/(1−r). For infinite series (|r| < 1): S∞ = a₁/(1−r). Your $1000 at 5%: if you kept it forever earning 5%, the present value of all future earnings = 1000/(1−0.05) = $20,000!
theorem.class11.geometric_sum.title: Sum of First n Terms of a Geometric Series
The midpoints of both diagonals of a parallelogram coincide.
This is why a crane's parallelogram linkage keeps the load level — the diagonals always intersect at their midpoints, maintaining the same relative position regardless of arm angle!
M = (3.5, 4.5). The other diagonal BD shares the same midpoint.
Parallelogram with A(1,2), B(4,3), C(6,7). Find midpoint of diagonal AC.
🏗️ You're a structural engineer designing a parallelogram linkage for a construction crane. The four vertices of the parallelogram ABCD have position vectors ã, b⃗, c⃗, d⃗ from the origin.
📐 The midpoint M₁ of diagonal AC has position vector (ã + c⃗)/2 — the average of the two endpoint vectors.
📐 The midpoint M₂ of diagonal BD has position vector (b⃗ + d⃗)/2.
🧮 In a parallelogram, AB = DC. In vectors: b⃗ − ã = c⃗ − d⃗ → b⃗ + d⃗ = ã + c⃗ → (ã + c⃗)/2 = (b⃗ + d⃗)/2. The midpoints are identical — the diagonals bisect each other!
Diagonals of a Parallelogram Bisect Each Other
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.finalF: The remainder when dividing by (x−c) equals p(c).
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.keyIns: This theorem lets you evaluate polynomials at a point without substituting! It's the foundation of synthetic division and is used in computer graphics for fast polynomial evaluation (Horner's method).
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.practi: p(x) = x² − 5x + 6. Find the remainder when divided by (x−2).
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.step1_: 📈 You're a data scientist fitting a polynomial to temperature data. You need to know the value at a specific time t = c. The Remainder Theorem lets you find it without computing the whole polynomial!
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.step2_: 📐 Substitute x = c into the identity: p(c) = (c−c)q(c) + r. The (c−c) term becomes zero.
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.step3_: 🧮 Since c−c = 0, the term (c−c)q(c) = 0 × q(c) = 0. Therefore p(c) = r. The remainder when dividing p(x) by (x−c) equals p(c) — the polynomial evaluated at x = c!
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.step4_: ✅ This theorem is the basis of synthetic division — a shortcut that divides polynomials using only coefficients. It's vastly faster for high-degree polynomials and is used in computer graphics for Bézier curve evaluation (Horner's method reduces O(n²) to O(n))!
theorem.class11.remainder_theorem.title: Remainder Theorem
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.final: The segment joining midpoints of non-parallel sides is half the sum of parallel sides.
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.keyIn: Bridge designers use this theorem: the midline of a trapezoidal bridge girder equals the average of the top and bottom widths. It's also used in numerical integration (trapezoidal rule) where the average of endpoints approximates the integral!
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.pract: Trapezium with AB=8cm, DC=12cm (parallel). Find the length of the segment joining midpoints of non-parallel sides.
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.step1: 🌉 You're a civil engineer designing a bridge with a trapezoidal cross-section. The top deck AB is parallel to the bottom base DC. You need to find the length of the mid-span support beam.
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.step2: 📐 M₁ is the midpoint of side AD, M₂ is the midpoint of side BC — these are the endpoints of your mid-span beam.
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.step3: 🧮 M₁M₂ = ½[(b⃗ − ã) + (c⃗ − d⃗)] = ½(AB + DC). The sum of the parallel side vectors, halved.
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.step4: ✅ Since AB ∥ DC, M₁M₂ is parallel to both. Its length = ½(|AB| + |DC|). For a bridge with top=10m and bottom=16m: midspan = ½(10+16) = 13m. This is why the trapezoidal rule in calculus uses the average of endpoints!
theorem.class11.trapezium_midpoint.title: Trapezium Midpoint Theorem
theorem.class11.triangle_midpoint.finalF: The segment joining midpoints of two sides is parallel to and half the third side.
theorem.class11.triangle_midpoint.keyIns: This theorem is the foundation of the intercept theorem and is used in architecture to create truss structures. It also explains why the median of a triangle divides it into two equal areas!
theorem.class11.triangle_midpoint.practi: Triangle with A(0,0), B(6,0), C(2,4). Find the length of M₁M₂ (midpoints of CA and BC).
theorem.class11.triangle_midpoint.step1_: 🔺 You're a carpenter building a roof truss. The triangle has vertices A(ã), B(b⃗), C(c⃗). You need to find the beam that connects the midpoints of two sides.
theorem.class11.triangle_midpoint.step2_: 📐 M₁ is the midpoint of CA: (ã + c⃗)/2. M₂ is the midpoint of BC: (b⃗ + c⃗)/2. These are the truss's connection points.
theorem.class11.triangle_midpoint.step3_: 🧮 M₁M₂ = M₂ − M₁ = (b⃗ + c⃗)/2 − (ã + c⃗)/2 = ½(b⃗ − ã) = ½AB. The c⃗ terms cancel!
theorem.class11.triangle_midpoint.step4_: ✅ M₁M₂ = ½AB. This means: (1) M₁M₂ is parallel to AB (scalar multiple), and (2) its length is exactly half of AB. Your roof truss beam is perfectly positioned at half-scale of the base!
theorem.class11.triangle_midpoint.title: Triangle Midpoint Theorem
theorem.class11.trig_identities.finalFor: Double-angle formulas derived from the fundamental law.
theorem.class11.trig_identities.keyInsig: Double and triple angle formulas are essential in physics (harmonic oscillators, wave interference), engineering (Fourier series decomposing signals into frequencies), and even music synthesis (generating overtones from base frequencies)
theorem.class11.trig_identities.practice: sin(30°) = 0.5. Find sin(60°) using the double-angle formula.
theorem.class11.trig_identities.step1_de: 🧭 Navigation: If you turn 30° clockwise vs 30° counterclockwise, the eastward component is the same! By the fundamental law with α=0: cos(−β) = cos 0 cos β + sin 0 sin β = cos β.
theorem.class11.trig_identities.step2_de: 🔄 Using co-ratios: sin(α+β) = cos(π/2−(α+β)) = cos[(π/2−α)−β] = cos(π/2−α)cos β + sin(π/2−α)sin β = sin α cos β + cos α sin β.
theorem.class11.trig_identities.step3_de: 🧮 Let β = α in sin(α+β): sin(2α) = sin α cos α + cos α sin α = 2 sin α cos α. For cos: cos(2α) = cos²α − sin²α = 2cos²α − 1 = 1 − 2sin²α.
theorem.class11.trig_identities.step4_de: 🔊 Audio engineers use this: sin(3α) = sin(2α+α) = sin(2α)cos α + cos(2α)sin α = (2 sin α cos α)cos α + (1 − 2 sin²α)sin α = 2 sin α(1−sin²α) + sin α − 2 sin³α = 3 sin α − 4 sin³α. This generates the third harmonic in synthesizers!
theorem.class11.trig_identities.title: Trigonometric Identities (Double & Triple Angle)
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.finalFor: Basic integral formulas derived from reverse differentiation.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.keyInsig: Integration is the inverse of differentiation. Every differentiation rule gives an integration rule! The power rule d/dx xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) = xⁿ reverses to ∫ xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C. This is the foundation of all integral calculus.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.practice: ∫ x³ dx = ? (Evaluate at x=2 minus x=0)
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.step1_de: 🏗️ A civil engineer calculating the volume of an irregular foundation uses integration. If the cross-sectional area is A(x) = x², the volume from x=0 to x=3 is ∫₀³ x² dx = [x³/3]₀³ = 9 cubic meters.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.step2_de: 📐 The power rule fails when n = −1 (would give division by zero). But d/dx[ln x] = 1/x, so ∫ 1/x dx = ln|x| + C. This is the bridge between algebraic and logarithmic functions.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.step3_de: 🧮 Differentiate aˣ/ln a: the derivative is aˣ·ln a / ln a = aˣ. Therefore ∫ aˣ dx = aˣ/ln a + C. For the natural exponential: ∫ eˣ dx = eˣ + C (since ln e = 1).
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.step4_de: ✅ Integration is linear: the integral of a sum is the sum of integrals, and constants factor out. This is why ∫ (3x² + 2x + 1) dx = 3·x³/3 + 2·x²/2 + x + C = x³ + x² + x + C.
theorem.class12.basic_integrals.title: Basic Indefinite Integrals
theorem.class12.circle_general.finalForm: The general equation represents a circle with center (−g, −f) and radius √(g²+f²−c).
theorem.class12.circle_general.keyInsigh: By completing the square, any equation x² + y² + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0 can be rewritten as a circle. If g²+f²−c > 0, it’s a real circle; if equal to 0, it’s a point circle; if negative, the equation has no real graph. This is how GPS receivers determine your position from three circle equations!
theorem.class12.circle_general.step1_det: 🗺️ A GPS receiver detects signals from three satellites. Each satellite’s signal defines a circle: x² + y² + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0. The receiver needs to find the center and radius of each circle to triangulate your position.
theorem.class12.circle_general.step2_det: 📐 Separate the x and y terms, moving c to the right side.
theorem.class12.circle_general.step3_det: 🧮 Add g² (to complete (x+g)²) and f² (to complete (y+f)²). Balance by subtracting g² and f² on the same side.
theorem.class12.circle_general.step3_lab: Complete the square for x and y
theorem.class12.circle_general.step4_det: ✅ This matches the standard circle form (x−h)² + (y−k)² = r² where h = −g, k = −f, r = √(g²+f²−c). The center is (−g, −f) and radius is √(g²+f²−c). For a real circle, we need g²+f²−c > 0!
theorem.class12.circle_general.title: General Equation of a Circle
theorem.class12.circle_tangent.finalForm: Equation of the tangent to the circle at point P(x₁, y₁).
theorem.class12.circle_tangent.keyInsigh: The tangent to a circle at a point is perpendicular to the radius at that point. The formula x₁x + y₁y + g(x + x₁) + f(y + y₁) + c = 0 is derived by applying the slope condition. This is known as "T = 0" — replace x² → x₁x, y² → y₁y, x → (x+x₁)/2, y → (y+y₁)/2!
theorem.class12.circle_tangent.practice_: Circle x²+y²=25 at point (3,4). Tangent slope = −x₁/y₁ = −3/4 = ?
theorem.class12.circle_tangent.step1_det: 🎯 A machinist needs to cut a gear tooth that makes contact at a precise point on a circular gear. The cutting tool must follow the tangent line at the contact point.
theorem.class12.circle_tangent.step1_lab: Find the slope by implicit differentiation
theorem.class12.circle_tangent.step2_det: 📐 Differentiate implicitly: 2x + 2y·y' + 2g + 2f·y' = 0. Solve for y': dy/dx = −(x+g)/(y+f).
theorem.class12.circle_tangent.step3_det: 🧮 At point P(x₁, y₁), substitute into dy/dx: slope m = −(x₁+g)/(y₁+f). The tangent line in point-slope form: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁).
theorem.class12.circle_tangent.step4_det: ✅ Cross-multiply and rearrange. The result is the elegant T = 0 formula: replace x² → x₁x, y² → y₁y, 2gx → g(x+x₁), 2fy → f(y+y₁), c → c. The tangent equation is written without computing the derivative explicitly!
theorem.class12.circle_tangent.title: Tangent to a Circle at a Given Point
theorem.class12.concurrency_altitudes.fi: The three altitudes of a triangle always intersect at a single point (the orthocenter).
theorem.class12.concurrency_altitudes.ke: The orthocenter is the intersection of all three altitudes. In an acute triangle it lies inside; in an obtuse triangle it lies outside! This concurrency is why the three altitudes of a triangle always meet — a remarkable geometric fact used in surveying to locate positions.
theorem.class12.concurrency_altitudes.pr: Think about what it means for three lines to meet at one point
Hint: It starts with "con" and relates to lines of elevation.
Triangle with vertices (0,0), (4,0), (2,5). The three altitudes meet at a single point. What is this property called?
theorem.class12.concurrency_altitudes.st: 🏔️ A surveyor is mapping a triangular plot of land. To find the orthocenter (where all lines of elevation meet), they need to prove the three altitudes intersect at one point.
📐 The slope of BC is (y₃−y₂)/(x₃−x₂). Altitude AD ∦ BC, so its slope is the negative reciprocal: m_AD = −(x₃−x₂)/(y₃−y₂).
🧮 Using point-slope form with point A(x₁, y₁): y − y₁ = m_AD(x − x₁). Rearranging: (x₃−x₂)x + (y₃−y₂)y − x₁(x₃−x₂) − y₁(y₃−y₂) = 0.

✅ Write similar equations for BE and CF. Place their coefficients in a determinant. Adding R₂ + R₃ to R₁ makes the first row zero (since the sums of coefficients from each row cancel). A zero row means the determinant = 0, proving the lines are concurrent at the orthocenter!
theorem.class12.concurrency_altitudes.ti: Concurrency of Altitudes in a Triangle
theorem.class12.concurrency_bisectors.fi: The perpendicular bisectors of a triangle's sides intersect at the circumcenter.
theorem.class12.concurrency_bisectors.ke: The circumcenter is the center of the circle that passes through all three vertices. It's the intersection of the three perpendicular bisectors. This point is equidistant from all vertices — explaining why you can always draw a unique circle through any three non-collinear points!
theorem.class12.concurrency_bisectors.pr: What is the center of the circumscribed circle called?
theorem.class12.concurrency_bisectors.st: 📍 GPS triangulation: Three satellite positions A, B, C are known. To find your location, construct the perpendicular bisectors of AB and BC — their intersection is your position (circumcenter)!
📐 The right bisector of BC passes through the midpoint with slope perpendicular to BC.
🧮 Multiplying through and rearranging gives the standard linear equation. Note the constant terms involve squares of coordinates.
Simplify and write in standard form

✅ Write the equations for all three bisectors. The coefficient determinant has its first row become zero after adding R₂ + R₃. Zero determinant = concurrency. The intersection point (circumcenter) is equidistant from all three vertices — the center of the circumscribed circle!
theorem.class12.concurrency_medians.fina: The three medians of a triangle intersect at the centroid — the triangle’s center of mass.
theorem.class12.concurrency_medians.keyI: The centroid divides each median in a 2:1 ratio from the vertex to the midpoint. It’s the center of mass of the triangle — if you cut a triangle out of cardboard, it balances perfectly at the centroid! Engineers use this to find the center of gravity of triangular structures.
theorem.class12.concurrency_medians.prac: Triangle with vertices (0,0), (6,0), (3,6). Find the centroid x-coordinate.
theorem.class12.concurrency_medians.step: ⚖️ An architect is designing a triangular steel truss for a bridge roof. The center of mass (centroid) is where the medians intersect — this is where the main support column should be placed.
Median AD: vertex A to midpoint of BC
📐 The median AD passes through A(x₁, y₁) and the midpoint of BC. Using the two-point formula.
🧮 Cross-multiply and simplify to get a standard linear form.

✅ Write all three median equations. Their coefficient determinant becomes zero after adding rows, proving concurrency. The intersection point G = ((x₁+x₂+x₃)/3, (y₁+y₂+y₃)/3) — the centroid. This is also the average of the vertices, making it the center of mass!
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.finalFo: An ellipse with foci at (±c, 0) where c² = a²−b².
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.keyInsi: An ellipse is the set of points where the sum of distances to two foci is constant (2a). This is why planetary orbits are elliptical with the Sun at one focus (Kepler’s First Law). The flattening depends on eccentricity e = c/a = √(1−b²/a²)!
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.practic: Ellipse x²/25 + y²/9 = 1. Find c (distance from center to focus).
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.step1_d: 🌍 Johannes Kepler discovered that planets orbit the Sun in elliptical paths. The Sun is at one focus. The sum of distances from any point on the ellipse to the two foci is constant — the length of the major axis.
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.step1_l: Definition: sum of distances to foci = 2a
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.step2_d: 📐 Place foci at F₁(−c, 0) and F₂(c, 0). For any point P(x,y) on the ellipse: √[(x+c)²+y²] + √[(x−c)²+y²] = 2a.
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.step2_l: Set foci at (±c, 0) and point P(x, y)
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.step3_d: 🧮 Isolate one radical, square both sides, isolate the remaining radical, square again. After simplifying: (a²−c²)x² + a²y² = a²(a²−c²).
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.step4_d: ✅ Define b² = a²−c². Then b²x² + a²y² = a²b² → x²/a² + y²/b² = 1. For Earth’s orbit: a ≈ 149.6 million km, b ≈ 149.5 million km (nearly circular, e ≈ 0.017). Mars has e ≈ 0.093 — noticeably more elliptical!
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.step4_l: Let b² = a²−c² to get standard form
theorem.class12.ellipse_standard.title: Standard Equation of an Ellipse
theorem.class12.fundamental_theorem_calc: The definite integral equals the difference of the antiderivative at the bounds.

The FTC is the single most important theorem in calculus! It connects the two central concepts: differentiation and integration are inverse operations. This is why we can compute areas by finding antiderivatives — a profound link between geometry and algebra.
∫₁³ (2x) dx. Find F(3) − F(1) where F(x) = x².
🏔️ A geologist is calculating the volume of sediment in a river delta. The cross-sectional area varies with distance x from the shore. Define A(x) = ∫ₐˣ f(t) dt as the accumulated area from a to x.
📐 By definition: A'(x) = lim[A(x+h)−A(x)]/h as h→0. But A(x+h)−A(x) = ∫ₓˣ⁺ʰ f(t) dt ≈ f(x)·h by the Mean Value Theorem. So A'(x) = f(x). The area function is an antiderivative of f!
🧮 Since A'(x) = f(x), any antiderivative F of f differs from A by a constant: F(x) = A(x) + C. Then F(b)−F(a) = A(b)−A(a).

✅ But A(a) = ∫ₐᵃ f(t) dt = 0, so F(b)−F(a) = A(b) = ∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx. The definite integral equals the antiderivative evaluated at the bounds. The river delta volume: if A'(x) = 2x (linear increase), volume from x=0 to x=5 = [x²]₀⁵ = 25 cubic meters!
theorem.class12.homogeneous_second_degre: Every homogeneous second-degree equation represents a pair of lines through the origin.

A homogeneous equation ax² + 2hxy + by² = 0 has all terms of degree 2. It always factors into two linear equations, meaning it represents two straight lines through the origin. If h² − ab > 0, the lines are distinct and real — used in optics to analyze light cone sections!
Yes! x² − y² = (x−y)(x+y) = 0 is two lines through the origin: x = y and x = −y.
x² − y² = (x−y)(x+y) = 0 gives lines x=y and x=−y
Does x² − y² = 0 factor into two lines?
🔦 A physicist is analyzing a beam of light spreading from a point source. The light cone is described by a homogeneous equation: ax² + 2hxy + by² = 0. The goal is to factor it into two distinct rays.
📐 The key insight: complete a perfect square by adding and subtracting h²y².
🧮 The first three terms form (ax + hy)². The remaining terms become −y²(h²−ab).

✅ As a difference of squares: (ax + hy)² − (y√(h²−ab))² = 0. This factors into two linear equations: ax + y(h ± √(h²−ab)) = 0. Both pass through the origin (no constant term). If h² > ab: two distinct real lines. If h² = ab: one repeated line. If h² < ab: complex (imaginary) lines.
theorem.class12.hyperbola_standard.final: A hyperbola with foci at (±c, 0) where c² = a²+b².
theorem.class12.hyperbola_standard.keyIn: A hyperbola is the set of points where the difference of distances to two foci is constant (2a). Unlike the ellipse (sum is constant), the hyperbola has two separate branches. Used in navigation (LORAN systems) where the difference in signal arrival times from two stations locates a ship!
theorem.class12.hyperbola_standard.pract: Hyperbola x²/16 − y²/9 = 1. Find c (focus distance from center).
theorem.class12.hyperbola_standard.step1: 🚢 A ship uses LORAN navigation: two radio stations at F₁ and F₂ transmit synchronized signals. The ship measures the difference in arrival times. The locus of constant time difference is a hyperbola with foci at the stations.
Definition: difference of distances to foci = 2a
theorem.class12.hyperbola_standard.step2: 📐 The absolute difference of distances to F₁(−c,0) and F₂(c,0) equals 2a. Choose the branch where PF₁ > PF₂.
Set foci at (±c, 0) and point P(x, y)
theorem.class12.hyperbola_standard.step3: 🧮 Same double-squaring technique as the ellipse. But the sign difference (subtraction vs addition) leads to a different final form.
Square twice (same process as ellipse but sign differs)
theorem.class12.hyperbola_standard.step4: ✅ Define b² = c²−a². The standard form x²/a² − y²/b² = 1 has two branches (left and \). Asymptotes: y = ±(b/a)x. The LORAN ship: if stations are 100km apart (2c=100) and the constant difference is 60km (2a=60), then b² = 50²−30² = 1600, and the ship lies on x²/900 − y²/1600 = 1!
Let b² = c²−a² to get standard form
theorem.class12.hyperbola_standard.title: Standard Equation of a Hyperbola
theorem.class12.inverse_trig_derivatives: Derivatives of inverse trig functions using implicit differentiation.

Inverse trig derivatives appear in many integration problems (∫ 1/√(1−x²) dx = sin⁻¹ x + C). They're also essential in physics for calculating angles from ratios, like finding the launch angle of a projectile from its velocity components!

🎯 A ballistics expert measures the ratio of vertical to horizontal velocity (x) and needs the angle. If x = 0.5, then y = sin⁻¹(0.5) = 30°. Implicit differentiation finds how the angle changes with the ratio.
Let y = sin⁻¹ x ⇒ x = sin y
📐 Using implicit differentiation: d/dx(x) = d/dx(sin y) → 1 = cos y · dy/dx → dy/dx = 1/cos y.
🧮 Using the identity cos²y + sin²y = 1: cos y = √(1−sin²y) = √(1−x²). Therefore d/dx sin⁻¹ x = 1/√(1−x²).
✅ For tan⁻¹: differentiate x = tan y → 1 = sec²y·dy/dx. Since sec²y = 1 + tan²y = 1 + x², we get d/dx tan⁻¹ x = 1/(1+x²). These formulas are the key to integrating rational functions!
theorem.class12.parabola_standard.finalF: A parabola with focus at (a, 0) and directrix x = −a.
theorem.class12.parabola_standard.keyIns: A parabola is the set of points equidistant from a fixed point (focus) and a fixed line (directrix). This reflective property is why parabolic mirrors concentrate parallel light rays at the focus — used in telescopes, satellite dishes, and solar cookers!
theorem.class12.parabola_standard.practi: y² = 4ax. Solve for a from 4a = coefficient.
theorem.class12.parabola_standard.step1_: 📡 A satellite dish antenna has a parabolic shape. Signals arriving parallel to the axis reflect to the focus where the receiver is placed. The definition: every point P on the parabola is equidistant from the focus F and the directrix line.
Definition: distance to focus = distance to directrix
theorem.class12.parabola_standard.step2_: 📐 Choose coordinate axes so the focus F = (a, 0) and the directrix is x = −a. The distance from P(x,y) to F is √[(x−a)²+y²]. The distance to the directrix is |x−(−a)| = |x + a|.
Place focus at (a, 0) and directrix x = −a
theorem.class12.parabola_standard.step3_: 🧮 Square both sides (both are non-negative): (x−a)² + y² = (x+a)².
theorem.class12.parabola_standard.step4_: ✅ Expand: x²−2ax+a²+y² = x²+2ax+a². Cancel x² and a² on both sides. Solve: y² = 4ax. This is the standard equation of a parabola opening to the right. For satellite dishes: the receiver is at the focus (a, 0) and the dish follows y² = 4ax!
theorem.class12.parabola_standard.title: Standard Equation of a Parabola
theorem.class12.parabola_tangent.finalFo: Condition for tangency: substitute y = mx + c into y² = 4ax and set discriminant = 0.
theorem.class12.parabola_tangent.keyInsi: A line y = mx + c is tangent to y² = 4ax if c = a/m. This means the slope form of the tangent is y = mx + a/m. For a given slope m, there are exactly two parallel tangents (one on each side of the parabola), except for the vertical tangent at the vertex!
theorem.class12.parabola_tangent.practic: Parabola y² = 8x. Line slope m = 2. Find c for tangency (c = a/m).
theorem.class12.parabola_tangent.step1_d: 🎯 An architect is designing a parabolic arch and needs to find the tangent line at a point to calculate the support beam angle. Substitute the line equation y = mx + c into the parabola y² = 4ax.
theorem.class12.parabola_tangent.step2_d: 📐 Expand: m²x² + 2mcx + c² = 4ax. Rearrange: m²x² + 2(mc−2a)x + c² = 0. This is a quadratic in x.
theorem.class12.parabola_tangent.step3_d: 🧮 For tangency, the line must intersect at exactly one point. This means the quadratic has a double root: its discriminant D = b²−4ac must be zero.
theorem.class12.parabola_tangent.step4_d: ✅ Expand: 4(m²c²−4amc+4a²) − 4m²c² = 0 → −16amc + 16a² = 0 → mc = a → c = a/m. The tangent equation in slope form: y = mx + a/m. This formula allows architects to find the support beam angle at any point on a parabolic arch!
theorem.class12.parabola_tangent.title: Tangent to a Parabola
theorem.class12.power_rule.finalFormulaD: The derivative of xⁿ is n·xⁿ⁻¹ for any positive integer n.
theorem.class12.power_rule.keyInsight: The power rule is the single most used differentiation formula! It transforms polynomials into simpler ones: each term's exponent drops by 1 and becomes the new coefficient. Used everywhere from physics (velocity = derivative of position) to economics (marginal cost from total cost).
theorem.class12.power_rule.practice_hint: d/dx xⁿ = n·xⁿ⁻¹, so d/dx x⁵ = 5·x⁴
theorem.class12.power_rule.step1_detail: 📈 A rocket scientist needs to calculate the velocity of a spacecraft at every instant. Position s(t) = t³. By definition, velocity = derivative = limit of (s(t+h)−s(t))/h as h→0.
theorem.class12.power_rule.step2_detail: 📐 Expand (x+h)ⁿ using the binomial theorem. The terms are: xⁿ + n·xⁿ⁻¹·h + n(n−1)/2!·xⁿ⁻²·h² + ... + hⁿ.
theorem.class12.power_rule.step3_detail: 🧮 Subtract xⁿ and divide by h. The xⁿ cancels, and every remaining term contains h. Factor h: the numerator becomes h[n·xⁿ⁻¹ + ... + hⁿ⁻¹]. Cancel h with denominator.
theorem.class12.power_rule.step4_detail: ✅ As h→0, all terms with h vanish, leaving only n·xⁿ⁻¹. For the rocket: if s(t) = t³, velocity v(t) = 3t². At t=5 seconds: v = 3×25 = 75 m/s. The power rule makes differentiation instant!
theorem.class12.power_rule.title: Power Rule for Differentiation
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.finalForm: The derivative of sin x is cos x.
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.keyInsigh: This is the foundation of all trigonometric calculus! The derivative of sine is cosine because the slope of sin x at any point equals the value of cos x at that point. This relationship is what makes simple harmonic motion (springs, pendulums) so elegant: d²x/dt² = −ω²x.
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.step1_det: 🎵 An audio engineer analyzing sound waves needs to know how the amplitude changes. A sine wave sin(440t) represents musical note A440. The rate of change gives the velocity of the speaker cone.
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.step2_det: 📐 Use the identity sin(A+B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B: sin(x+h) = sin x cos h + cos x sin h.
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.step3_det: 🧮 Factor: = lim[sin x(cos h − 1)/h + cos x(sin h)/h]. Two special limits emerge!
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.step4_det: ✅ The two fundamental limits: (1) lim(cos h − 1)/h = 0 (the graph of cos is flat at h=0), (2) lim(sin h)/h = 1 (the small-angle approximation). Result: d/dx sin x = sin x·0 + cos x·1 = cos x. Similarly, d/dx cos x = −sin x.
theorem.class12.sin_derivative.title: Derivative of Sine Function
theorem.class12.substitution_integrals.f: Standard integrals derived via trigonometric substitution.
theorem.class12.substitution_integrals.k: Trigonometric substitution transforms square roots into trigonometric functions! √(a²−x²) → x = a sin θ → a cos θ, eliminating the radical. This technique was used by Newton to derive the area of a circle: ∫ √(a²−x²) dx gives the area of a circular segment!
theorem.class12.substitution_integrals.p: Use the formula ∫ 1/√(a²−x²) dx = sin⁻¹(x/a) + C
theorem.class12.substitution_integrals.s: 📐 An astronomer is calculating the area of a circular telescope mirror of radius a. The integral ∫ √(a²−x²) dx appears. Substitute x = a sin θ, which converts the circle’s equation into a trigonometric form.
🧮 √(a²−x²) = √(a²−a²sin²θ) = a√(1−sin²θ) = a cos θ. The integral becomes ∫ a cos θ · a cos θ dθ = a² ∫ cos²θ dθ.
📐 Use cos²θ = (1+cos 2θ)/2. Integrate: a²[θ/2 + sin 2θ/4] + C = (a²/2)θ + (a²/4) sin 2θ + C.

✅ Substituting back: ∫√(a²−x²)dx = (a²/2)sin⁻¹(x/a) + (x√(a²−x²))/2 + C. This formula gives the area of a circular segment of height x — used in civil engineering for water flow in partially filled pipes!
theorem.class9.angle_between_lines.final: Find the acute angle between two intersecting lines from their slopes.
theorem.class9.angle_between_lines.keyIn: Road engineers use this to design safe intersection angles — a 90° crossing is safest because it maximizes visibility in all directions!
theorem.class9.angle_between_lines.pract: m₁ = 1, m₂ = 2. Find the acute angle between them.
theorem.class9.angle_between_lines.step1: 🔄 Two roads cross at an intersection. One has a gentle slope m₁ = 0.5 (gentle hill), the other is steeper m₂ = 2.
theorem.class9.angle_between_lines.step2: When these lines cross, they form a triangle with the x-axis. The exterior angle of this triangle equals the sum of the two opposite interior angles: α = β + θ.
theorem.class9.angle_between_lines.step3: θ = α − β. Take tan of both sides: tan θ = tan(α − β). Using the tangent subtraction formula: tan(α−β) = (tan α − tan β)/(1 + tan α × tan β).
theorem.class9.angle_between_lines.step4: Since tan α = m₂ and tan β = m₁: tan θ = (m₂ − m₁)/(1 + m₁m₂). Take absolute value for the acute angle. If tan θ = 0, lines are parallel. If 1 + m₁m₂ = 0, lines are perpendicular!
theorem.class9.angle_between_lines.title: Angle Between Two Lines
theorem.class9.change_base.finalFormulaD: Convert a logarithm from one base to any other base.
theorem.class9.change_base.keyInsight: This is THE most practical log law because it lets you compute ANY log with just the log₁₀ or ln button on your calculator!
theorem.class9.change_base.practice_erro: log_b m = log_a m / log_a b
theorem.class9.change_base.practice_expl: log₂(100) ≈ 6.64. This formula lets you compute any log with common logs!
theorem.class9.change_base.practice_ques: log₁₀(100) = 2. Find log₂(100) using change of base.
theorem.class9.change_base.step1_detail: 📱 You're programming a calculator that only has log₁₀ (common log) and ln (natural log) buttons, but you need to compute log₂(8). This is the classic problem the Change of Base Law solves.
theorem.class9.change_base.step2_detail: Convert: m = b^x. We want to express this in terms of a known base a. Taking log_a of both sides: log_a m = log_a(b^x). Now we can use the Power Law in reverse!
theorem.class9.change_base.step3_detail: log_a m = x · log_a b. The exponent x "pulls down" in front. Now isolate x: x = log_a m / log_a b.
theorem.class9.change_base.step4_detail: Since x = log_b m, we get: log_b m = log_a m / log_a b. So log₂(8) = log₁₀(8) / log₁₀(2) = 0.9031 / 0.3010 = 3. Indeed, 2³ = 8.
theorem.class9.change_base.title: Change of Base Law
theorem.class9.distance_formula.finalFor: Calculate the distance between any two points on a coordinate plane.
theorem.class9.distance_formula.keyInsig: This formula is the GPS backbone: your phone calculates distance between satellites and your position using this exact formula in 3D!
theorem.class9.distance_formula.step1_de: 🗺️ You're a city planner. Point P₁(1, 2) is the library and P₂(7, 6) is the hospital. You need the straight-line distance between them to plan a new road.
theorem.class9.distance_formula.step2_de: Draw lines from P₁ and P₂ to the x-axis. These create a right triangle. The horizontal leg runs from x₁ to x₂, and the vertical leg from y₁ to y₂.
theorem.class9.distance_formula.step4_de: This formula is the GPS backbone: your phone calculates distance between satellites and your position using this exact formula in 3D!
theorem.class9.distance_formula.title: The Distance Formula
School library rule: you need a library card (A) AND (either a valid ID OR a teacher note). This is the same as (card AND ID) OR (card AND note).
🏫 School library rule: you need a library card (A) AND (either a valid ID OR a teacher note) (B ∪ C).
x ∈ A AND x ∈ (B ∪ C). That means x ∈ A AND (x ∈ B OR x ∈ C).
P AND (Q OR R) = (P AND Q) OR (P AND R). So: (x ∈ A AND x ∈ B) OR (x ∈ A AND x ∈ C).
x ∈ (A ∩ B) OR x ∈ (A ∩ C) means x ∈ (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C). So A ∩ (B ∪ C) ⊆ (A ∩ B) ∪ (A ∩ C). The reverse direction similarly proves equality.
Union distributes over intersection, like multiplication over addition.
Just like 2×(3+4) = 2×3 + 2×4 in arithmetic, set union distributes over intersection — it's the same distributive law!
🧩 Think of a school prize: winners get a free period (A) OR they get both extra recess AND homework pass (B ∩ C).
x ∈ A OR x ∈ (B ∩ C). That means either x ∈ A, OR (x ∈ B AND x ∈ C).
(P OR Q) AND (P OR R) is logically equivalent to P OR (Q AND R). So: (x ∈ A OR x ∈ B) AND (x ∈ A OR x ∈ C).
(x ∈ A ∪ B) AND (x ∈ A ∪ C) means x ∈ (A ∪ B) ∩ (A ∪ C). So A ∪ (B ∩ C) ⊆ (A ∪ B) ∩ (A ∪ C). The reverse containment also holds. Just like 2×(3+4) = 2×3 + 2×4!
The intersection operation is also associative.
A student in Art Club, Music Club, AND Drama Club — being in all three doesn't depend on how you group the clubs. Intersection is just "is in ALL of these sets."
🎭 Three clubs at school: Art Club A, Music Club B, and Drama Club C. A triple threat student does all three. Let y ∈ (A ∩ B) ∩ C.
y ∈ (A ∩ B) ∩ C means y ∈ (A ∩ B) AND y ∈ C. And y ∈ (A ∩ B) means y ∈ A AND y ∈ B. So: (y ∈ A AND y ∈ B) AND y ∈ C.
Logical AND is associative: (P AND Q) AND R = P AND (Q AND R). So (y ∈ A AND y ∈ B) AND y ∈ C = y ∈ A AND (y ∈ B AND y ∈ C).
y ∈ A AND (y ∈ B AND y ∈ C) means y ∈ A AND y ∈ (B ∩ C), which means y ∈ A ∩ (B ∩ C). Reverse containment proves equality. Just like union, intersection is associative!
theorem.class9.normal_form.finalFormulaD: Equation using perpendicular distance p from origin and inclination α.
theorem.class9.normal_form.keyInsight: This is particularly useful in physics and engineering for finding distances from a point to a line, and for describing wave fronts in optics.
theorem.class9.normal_form.practice_erro: x cos α + y sin α = p
theorem.class9.normal_form.practice_ques: α = 60°, p = 5. Find cos α and evaluate x cos α + y sin α at x=2, y=3.
theorem.class9.normal_form.step1_detail: 🧭 Imagine a line L and a perpendicular from the origin O to L meeting at point C. The distance OC = p, and the angle this perpendicular makes with the x-axis is α.
theorem.class9.normal_form.step2_detail: From right triangle OCA: cos α = p/OA → OA = p/cos α (x-intercept). From triangle OCB: sin α = p/OB → OB = p/sin α (y-intercept).
theorem.class9.normal_form.step3_detail: Using x/a + y/b = 1: x/(p/cos α) + y/(p/sin α) = 1 → (x cos α)/p + (y sin α)/p = 1.
theorem.class9.normal_form.step4_detail: x cos α + y sin α = p. The Normal Form! This is particularly useful in physics and engineering for finding distances from a point to a line.
theorem.class9.normal_form.title: Normal Form of a Line
theorem.class9.point_slope.finalFormulaD: Equation of a line through a point with a given slope.
theorem.class9.point_slope.keyInsight: This is incredibly useful in calculus, physics, and engineering when you know one point and the rate of change (slope).
theorem.class9.point_slope.practice_ques: Line through (1,2) with slope m = 4. Find y when x = 3.
theorem.class9.point_slope.step1_detail: 📍 You know a line passes through the point (2, 3) and has slope m = 1.5. You need the equation of the line to graph it on your digital map.
theorem.class9.point_slope.step2_detail: Let P(x, y) be any point on the line. With B(x₁, y₁) = (2, 3), the slope m = (y − y₁)/(x − x₁).
theorem.class9.point_slope.step3_detail: m(x − x₁) = y − y₁. This equation says "for any point on the line, the slope from (x₁, y₁) to (x, y) is always m."
theorem.class9.point_slope.step4_detail: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁). For our example: y − 3 = 1.5(x − 2) → y = 1.5x.
theorem.class9.point_slope.title: Point-Slope Form
theorem.class9.power_log.finalFormulaDes: The log of a power equals the exponent times the log of the base.
theorem.class9.power_log.keyInsight: Astronomers love logs: the distance to the farthest galaxies is 10²⁶ meters — log₁₀(10²⁶) = 26 × log₁₀(10) = 26. A 26 on the log scale is easy to write and compare!
theorem.class9.power_log.practice_explan: log₂(81) = 4 × 1.585 = 6.34. The power pulls down in front!
theorem.class9.power_log.step1_detail: 💰 You're an investor watching your money grow. Your initial investment of m = $3,000 grows at b = 2× per year. After n = 4 years, your money multiplies by 2^4. Let log_b m = x, meaning b^x = m.
theorem.class9.power_log.step2_detail: Convert m = b^x. Your initial $3,000 = 2^x means x = log₂(3000) ≈ 11.55 — the "doubling power" of your investment.
theorem.class9.power_log.step3_detail: Raise both sides to the n-th power: m^n = (b^x)^n. Using the power of a power rule: m^n = b^(nx). Your investment after 4 years: 3000^4? No — this is a mathematical power, not financial.
theorem.class9.power_log.step3_label: Raise both sides to power n
theorem.class9.power_log.step4_detail: Converting m^n = b^(nx) to log form: log_b(m^n) = nx. Substituting x = log_b m: log_b(m^n) = n log_b m. The exponent "pulls down" in front of the log!
theorem.class9.power_log.title: Power Law of Logarithms
theorem.class9.product_log.finalFormulaD: The log of a product equals the sum of the logs.
theorem.class9.product_log.keyInsight: The Richter scale uses this law: a magnitude 8 earthquake isn't twice as powerful as a magnitude 4 — it's 10,000 times! Because the scale is logarithmic, each whole number adds a factor of 10.
theorem.class9.product_log.step1_detail: 🧮 You're a sound engineer mixing a live concert. The microphone picks up sound at power m = 4 units, and the amplifier adds n = 8 units. To figure out the total gain, you reach for logarithms. Let log_b m = x and log_b n = y — these represent the "logarithmic power" of each signal.
theorem.class9.product_log.step2_detail: Converting back: m = b^x and n = b^y. In our concert, if b = 2 (binary amplification), then m = 2^x and n = 2^y. The exponential form reveals the actual multipliers hiding behind the log values.
theorem.class9.product_log.step3_detail: Now multiply m × n: mn = b^x · b^y. When multiplying powers with the same base, we ADD the exponents: mn = b^(x+y). This is the key insight — multiplication in the real world becomes addition in log world.
theorem.class9.product_log.step4_detail: Convert mn = b^(x+y) back to logarithmic form: log_b(mn) = x + y. Substitute x = log_b m and y = log_b n, and you get: log_b(mn) = log_b m + log_b n. The total sound power (product) equals the sum of the individual log powers.
theorem.class9.product_log.title: Product Law of Logarithms
theorem.class9.pythagorean_identities.fi: The Pythagorean Theorem restated in trigonometric form.
theorem.class9.pythagorean_identities.ke: From this one identity, we can derive: sec²θ − tan²θ = 1 and cosec²θ − cot²θ = 1. These are used in everything from signal processing to orbital mechanics!
theorem.class9.pythagorean_identities.pr: sin²(30°) + cos²(30°) = 1. Always 1, no matter the angle!
theorem.class9.pythagorean_identities.st: 📐 In any right triangle with legs a, b and hypotenuse c: a² + b² = c². This is the most famous relationship in all of geometry.
sin θ = a/c and cos θ = b/c. Square both: sin²θ = a²/c² and cos²θ = b²/c².
sin²θ + cos²θ = a²/c² + b²/c² = (a² + b²)/c² = c²/c² = 1. The triangle's legs and hypotenuse cancel out perfectly!
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 — it doesn't matter what θ is, this always holds true! From this, we can derive: sec²θ − tan²θ = 1 and cosec²θ − cot²θ = 1.
theorem.class9.quotient_identities.final: Tangent is sine divided by cosine; cotangent is the reciprocal.
theorem.class9.quotient_identities.keyIn: On a unit circle, the tangent at angle θ is the y-coordinate divided by the x-coordinate — the slope of the radius line!
theorem.class9.quotient_identities.pract: tan θ = sin θ / cos θ
theorem.class9.quotient_identities.step1: 🔺 You're standing at the base of a building, looking up at the top. The angle of elevation is θ. sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse = a/c. cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse = b/c. tan θ = opposite/adjacent = a/b.
theorem.class9.quotient_identities.step2: sin θ / cos θ = (a/c) / (b/c) = (a/c) × (c/b) = a/b. The hypotenuse (c) cancels out! And a/b is exactly tan θ.
theorem.class9.quotient_identities.step3: Similarly, cos θ / sin θ = b/a = cot θ. These are the Quotient Identities. They connect the primary ratios (sin, cos) to the secondary ones (tan, cot).
theorem.class9.quotient_identities.title: Quotient Trigonometric Identities
theorem.class9.quotient_log.finalFormula: The log of a quotient equals the difference of the logs.
theorem.class9.quotient_log.keyInsight: pH = −log[H⁺] uses this: a solution at pH 3 is 100× more acidic than pH 5, because 10^(5−3) = 10² = 100 on the logarithmic scale.
theorem.class9.quotient_log.practice_exp: log₁₀(10) = 1. The quotient law correctly gives us back 1!
theorem.class9.quotient_log.step1_detail: 🔬 You're a chemist measuring the concentration of a solution before and after dilution. The initial concentration is m = 32 M (molar) and after dilution it drops to n = 4 M. Let's set log_b m = x and log_b n = y.
theorem.class9.quotient_log.step2_detail: Convert to exponential form: m = b^x and n = b^y. With base b = 10 (common log), we have 32 = 10^x and 4 = 10^y. The exponents x and y are the powers of 10 that give us these concentrations.
theorem.class9.quotient_log.step3_detail: Divide m by n: m/n = b^x / b^y. When dividing powers with the same base, we SUBTRACT the exponents: m/n = b^(x−y). Division in the real world becomes subtraction in log world.
theorem.class9.quotient_log.step4_detail: Convert m/n = b^(x−y) to log form: log_b(m/n) = x − y. Substituting back: log_b(m/n) = log_b m − log_b n. The dilution factor in log terms is just the difference of the individual log concentrations.
theorem.class9.quotient_log.title: Quotient Law of Logarithms
theorem.class9.slope_intercept.finalForm: Equation of a straight line with slope m and y-intercept c.
theorem.class9.slope_intercept.keyInsigh: In business, this models linear cost functions: Total Cost = Variable Cost × Units + Fixed Cost. The slope is the cost per unit!
theorem.class9.slope_intercept.practice_: A line has slope m = 3 and intercept c = 2. What is y when x = 4?
theorem.class9.slope_intercept.step1_det: 📈 You're tracking your savings. You start with $100 (y-intercept c = 1 on a scaled graph) and save $2 per week (slope m = 2). The line shows your total savings over time.
theorem.class9.slope_intercept.step2_det: Let P(x, y) be any point on the line. The point where the line crosses the y-axis is A(0, c) — your starting savings at week 0.
theorem.class9.slope_intercept.step3_det: Slope m = (y − c) / (x − 0) = (y − c) / x. This means the rise over run from A to P equals the constant slope of the line.
theorem.class9.slope_intercept.step4_det: m = (y − c)/x → y − c = mx → y = mx + c. That's it! All straight lines can be written this way.
theorem.class9.slope_intercept.title: Slope-Intercept Form
theorem.class9.two_intercept_form.finalF: Equation of a line using its x-intercept a and y-intercept b.
theorem.class9.two_intercept_form.keyIns: Economists use this to graph budget constraints: if you spend $a on food and $b on housing, x/a + y/b = 1 shows all possible spending combinations!
theorem.class9.two_intercept_form.practi: x-intercept = 6, y-intercept = 3. Find y when x = 2.
theorem.class9.two_intercept_form.step1_: 🎯 A line crosses the x-axis at x = 4 (the x-intercept a = 4) and the y-axis at y = 3 (the y-intercept b = 3).
theorem.class9.two_intercept_form.step2_: Slope m = (b − 0)/(0 − a) = b/(−a) = −b/a = −3/4. The line goes down 3 for every 4 right.
theorem.class9.two_intercept_form.step3_: Using point (0, b): y − b = (−b/a)x → ay − ab = −bx → bx + ay = ab.
theorem.class9.two_intercept_form.step4_: (bx)/(ab) + (ay)/(ab) = 1 → x/a + y/b = 1. This elegant form shows the intercepts directly.
theorem.class9.two_intercept_form.title: Two-Intercept Form
theorem.class9.two_point_form.finalFormu: Equation of a line through two given points.
theorem.class9.two_point_form.keyInsight: Surveyors use this formula to map boundaries between measured points — the line connecting two measured coordinates!
theorem.class9.two_point_form.practice_q: Line through (0,0) and (2,6). Find y when x = 5.
theorem.class9.two_point_form.step1_deta: 🔗 You have two points A(1, 2) and B(4, 8). Only one straight line passes through both — the shortest path between them.
theorem.class9.two_point_form.step2_deta: Slope m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁) = (8 − 2)/(4 − 1) = 6/3 = 2. This line rises 2 units for every 1 unit horizontally.
theorem.class9.two_point_form.step3_deta: Now that we have m = 2, use the Point-Slope Form with either point: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁) → y − 2 = 2(x − 1).
theorem.class9.two_point_form.step4_deta: Substituting the slope formula directly gives: y − y₁ = ((y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁))(x − x₁). This is the Two-Point Form.
theorem.class9.two_point_form.title: Two-Point Form
theorem.class9.union_associative.finalFo: The union operation is associative — grouping doesn't matter.
theorem.class9.union_associative.keyInsi: The school fair analogy: three groups (food stall, games, raffle). The total combined team is the same no matter how you group them — union is just "is in at least one of these sets."
theorem.class9.union_associative.step1_d: 🏟️ Imagine three groups of students at a school fair: Group A runs the food stall, Group B runs games, and Group C runs the raffle. Let x be any student. If x is in (A ∪ B) ∪ C, then...
theorem.class9.union_associative.step2_d: x ∈ (A ∪ B) ∪ C means x ∈ (A ∪ B) OR x ∈ C. And x ∈ (A ∪ B) means x ∈ A OR x ∈ B. So overall: (x ∈ A OR x ∈ B) OR x ∈ C.
theorem.class9.union_associative.step3_d: The logical OR is associative — (P OR Q) OR R = P OR (Q OR R). So (x ∈ A OR x ∈ B) OR x ∈ C = x ∈ A OR (x ∈ B OR x ∈ C). The school fair doesn't care how you group the teams!
theorem.class9.union_associative.step4_d: x ∈ A OR (x ∈ B OR x ∈ C) means x ∈ A OR x ∈ (B ∪ C), which means x ∈ A ∪ (B ∪ C). The reverse containment proves equality. Thus (A ∪ B) ∪ C = A ∪ (B ∪ C). Grouping doesn't matter!
theorem.class9.union_associative.title: Associative Property of Union

The discriminant D = b²−4ac tells you everything: D > 0 means 2 real roots (arch crosses ground twice), D = 0 means 1 root (arch just touches ground), D < 0 means no real roots (arch never meets ground).

⏰ Final formula: T = constant × √(l/g). The constant turns out to be 2π (from solving the SHM equation for small angles). T = 2π√(l/g). In 1656, Christiaan Huygens built the first pendulum clock using this formula. A 1-meter pendulum gives T ≈ 2 seconds — perfect for a clock's tick-tock!
